scenarios.name: CROSS scenario
scenarios.label: The Tenova Variation Order Cascade
scenarios.scenarioDate: October 2026 — 15 months into construction, 14 months from commissioning target
scenarios.trigger: Single contract event cascading across all project roles

P1.tension: Programme continuity and 2027 date vs fiduciary discipline and legal precedent.
P1.timeHorizon: 21-day decision window; legal dispute could run 12–18 months.

options.label: Pay the full £31.4M under protest
options.summary: Programme continues uninterrupted. Legal dispute proceeds in parallel through the contract's dispute resolution mechanism.
options.risk: sets a precedent; the £14.9M may be unrecoverable if the dispute goes against Tata.

options.label: Pay only the agreed £16.5M (Factor 3)
options.summary: Dispute £14.9M through formal contract mechanism. Tenova may invoke suspension.
options.risk: programme slips 6–8 weeks on top of the 11-week extension; total slip could be 17–19 weeks; 2027 commissioning date is gone.

options.label: Negotiate a commercial settlement
options.summary: Split the difference — pay £23–25M as a full and final settlement, close the dispute, and keep Tenova cooperative for the remainder of the programme.
options.risk: paying more than Tata's legal position requires; sets precedent for future claims.

P1.summary: Tata's lawyers say withholding is defensible on Factors 1 and 2. But invoking the payment security clause would give Tenova the right to suspend construction for 6–8 weeks — and that suspension would become public. Rajesh must decide within 21 days.

P2.tension: Protecting the reskilling pipeline vs using the crisis as leverage for a structural decision the CEO needs to make anyway.
P2.timeHorizon: Next cohort starts in 6 weeks; decision needed in 10 days.

options.label: Escalate the trainer availability problem to the CEO as a commissioning risk
options.summary: Force a formal programme board decision on whether to mandate the IJmuiden secondments now, using the VO-112 evidence of industry-wide specialist scarcity as the justification. Turns a reskilling logistics problem into a strategic argument.

options.label: Absorb the 8-week delay quietly and compress later cohorts
options.summary: Manage the problem internally without escalating.
options.risk: each week of delay at this stage is harder to recover; the first cohort sets the pace for all subsequent ones.

options.label: Fast-track a direct hire of refractory specialists as EAF operator trainers
options.summary: Offer the same refractory engineers cited in Tenova's claim a parallel training role at Tata — they are on-site anyway and have the exact expertise the reskilling programme needs. Cost: ~£850K for a 6-month training facilitation contract.

P2.summary: The same refractory specialist shortage Tenova cited in VO-112 Factor 2 is being felt in Chris's reskilling pipeline — the external EAF training providers he was planning to use have had to delay Tata's cohort because their own trainers are committed to the same three simultaneous EAF projects. The reskilling timeline was already tight; the trainer availability crisis means the next scheduled cohort is now 8 weeks behind.

P3.tension: Site velocity and team relationships vs legal protection during an active commercial dispute.
P3.timeHorizon: 30-day refractory team redeployment threat; programme board meets in 5 days.

options.label: Operate the site formally under contract, documenting everything
options.summary: Issue all instructions in writing, require written acknowledgement. Protects Tata's legal position but slows decision velocity on site from hours to days.
options.risk: construction programme suffers from the bureaucratic overhead even before any formal suspension.

options.label: Maintain informal working relationship with Tenova's site team while dispute is resolved at commercial level
options.summary: Keep site velocity up; deal with the legal dispute through corporate channels without letting it infect the construction team relationship.
options.risk: informal working during a live dispute creates ambiguity about what has been agreed and for how much — any deviation becomes a new variation claim.

options.label: Accelerate parallel works that are not affected by the refractory dispute
options.summary: Shift resources to the scrap handling facility, LMF installation, and electrical infrastructure while the Tenova dispute is active. Partially decouples programme velocity from the dispute outcome.
options.risk: resource diversion may create quality risks on the accelerated work; ABB's schedule is not designed for acceleration.

P3.summary: Owen is contractually obligated to continue issuing instructions to Tenova, but Tenova's site team is operating under their own legal counsel's guidance — which means every instruction Owen issues is now being logged and potentially reserved as supporting evidence in the dispute. The site atmosphere has changed. Tenova's senior site manager has told Owen privately that if the £14.9M is not paid, the refractory team will be redeployed to the French EAF project within 30 days.

P4.tension: Cash discipline and contingency preservation vs programme continuity and grant transparency.
P4.timeHorizon: 21-day payment decision; grant covenant review cycle is quarterly.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, notify DBET proactively
options.summary: Full transparency; maintains grant relationship.
options.risk: DBET may require a formal programme review before releasing the next milestone payment — which could delay cash flow by 8–12 weeks.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, do not notify DBET unless required
options.summary: Only disclose if the overrun breaches a defined threshold in the grant agreement.
options.risk: if DBET later discovers Tata knew and didn't tell them, the relationship damage is severe.

options.label: Reject the full payment; reserve contingency for the agreed Factor 3 element only
options.summary: Do not consume contingency on disputed items. If Tenova suspends, the 6–8 week slip is a budget problem for a different period.
options.risk: programme slip costs are in some ways more expensive than the disputed amount — the indirect cost of a 6–8 week suspension (slab import extension, customer risk, staff costs) may exceed £14.9M.

P4.summary: The capital programme budget has a 7.5% contingency built in — approximately £93M against the £1.25B programme. VO-112 at £31.4M is 33% of that contingency. If Tata pays the full amount, 33% of its total programme contingency is consumed by a single contractor dispute at month 15 of a 29-month construction programme. There are 14 months left and multiple other programme risks still live.

P5.tension: Institutional accountability to members vs cooperative relationship needed for EAF operator terms.
P5.timeHorizon: Transition Board meets in 3 weeks; parliamentary question window is now.

options.label: Request a formal Transition Board agenda item on VO-112 and its workforce implications
options.summary: Forces public transparency; puts the commissioning delay and the non-local specialist labour question on the record.
options.risk: antagonises Tata at the worst possible time — when Tata needs contractor goodwill and the union needs EAF operator terms cooperation.

options.label: Accept the private briefing and monitor without escalating
options.summary: Trust the process; wait for the commercial dispute to resolve before making it a Transition Board issue.
options.risk: if the delay hardens and former workers who were counting on 2027 EAF jobs find out Alun knew and said nothing, it is a repeat of the blast furnace closure communications failure.

options.label: Raise it quietly through Stephen Kinnock (MP) as a parliamentary question
options.summary: Creates political pressure without directly confronting Tata; forces DBET to publicly confirm the programme status.
options.risk: parliamentary questions are public record; this is effectively option A but less controllable.

P5.summary: Alun will be briefed on VO-112 through his Transition Board membership. The 11-week programme extension means the EAF commissioning date is now formally at risk. This directly affects the timeline for EAF operator job creation — the moment redundant former steelworkers had been told to look forward to. The refractory labour claim (Factor 2) also highlights that Tenova is using non-local specialists for a key construction phase while Community Union has been pushing for more local hiring.

P6.tension: Board governance and director liability vs the media and political consequences of formal disclosure.
P6.timeHorizon: Legal opinion: 2–3 weeks; Indian exchange filing obligations: 48 hours if material.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as a material event and issue a proactive disclosure to DBET, Welsh Government, and Tata Steel Ltd's Indian exchange filings
options.summary: Full transparency; protects independent directors.
options.risk: disclosure triggers a media story — "Tata Steel Port Talbot EAF £31M over budget and 11 weeks late" is a damaging headline 14 months from commissioning.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as within normal programme contingency and not material — disclose only if and when the commercial dispute is resolved against Tata
options.summary: Programme-level decision; not a board-level material event.
options.risk: if the dispute escalates and it later emerges the board classified it as immaterial, independent director liability is real.

options.label: Commission a legal opinion on materiality and delay disclosure pending its conclusion
options.summary: Buys 2–3 weeks; ensures the board has defensible grounds for its decision.
options.risk: the delay itself may become an issue if DBET or Tata Group discovers VO-112 independently in the interim.

P6.summary: The Tata Steel UK board must assess whether £31.4M in disputed contract variation, plus an 11-week programme extension, constitutes a material event under UK company law, the grant agreement's disclosure obligations, or Tata Steel Ltd's listing obligations on the Indian stock exchanges.

P7.tension: Contract certainty vs the cost of acting on an unconfirmed programme slip.
P7.timeHorizon: Three scrap contracts have February 2028 termination clauses; renegotiation takes 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Renegotiate the three at-risk contracts now, before the slip is formally confirmed
options.summary: Tell the merchants the start date may move by 8–12 weeks; ask for a grace period clause in exchange for a modest price concession (~£2/tonne). Cost: ~£1.4M/yr on those contracts.
options.risk: if the programme recovers and commissioning holds at Q4 2027, Tata has paid a concession it didn't need to.

options.label: Wait for the VO-112 dispute to resolve before renegotiating
options.summary: Do not trigger a conversation with scrap merchants about a delay that has not been formally confirmed.
options.risk: by the time VO-112 is resolved (6–8 weeks), the merchants' own pricing windows may have closed — and renegotiating under time pressure will cost more.

options.label: Treat the three at-risk contracts as expendable and replace them with shorter-duration bridge contracts
options.summary: Accept the loss and sign 12-month spot-priced bridge agreements to cover the delay period. Cost: PSX spot prices are currently running ~£12/tonne above long-term contract rates.
options.risk: further delays compound the spot exposure; bridge contracts create a rolling renegotiation burden.

P7.summary: Kate's current contracting strategy assumed EAF commissioning in Q4 2027 and first full production in Q1 2028. Her long-term scrap contracts are structured around a January 2028 first delivery date. An 11-week slip pushes first production to approximately March–April 2028.

P8.tension: Contract security vs avoiding renegotiation of agreements that are currently on good terms.
P8.timeHorizon: PPA pricing trigger is Q4 2027 — formal amendment needs to be in place 8 weeks before trigger.

options.label: Issue a formal contract amendment notice to National Grid and the PPA counterparty
options.summary: Updates both agreements to reflect the revised timeline.
options.risk: formal amendments trigger renegotiation windows — National Grid may use the amendment to revise terms; the PPA counterparty may request price renegotiation on an agreement that is currently below market.

options.label: Do nothing contractually; treat both agreements as flexible within a 3-month window
options.summary: Most infrastructure contracts have implicit flexibility for minor schedule adjustments.
options.risk: if the slip exceeds the informal flexibility window and either party invokes strict contract terms, Tata is in breach — a worse position than having amended proactively.

options.label: Issue informal notice letters to both parties — acknowledging the potential slip, requesting confirmation of continued alignment, without triggering a formal amendment process
options.summary: Preserves optionality; puts both parties on notice without opening renegotiation.
options.risk: legally ambiguous — informal notice may or may not constitute sufficient notification under the contract terms.

P8.summary: National Grid committed to deliver the new high-voltage infrastructure to Port Talbot by end-2027 — aligned to the original EAF commissioning target. An 11-week slip moves Tata's requirement to approximately February–March 2028. This is actually positive for National Grid's programme (they now have more time), but it creates a contractual ambiguity: the current agreement specifies "ready by end 2027" — if Tata's commissioning moves, does the National Grid obligation move too, and does it require a formal contract amendment?

P9.tension: Community trust and transparency vs not getting ahead of a commercial dispute that may resolve in Tata's favour.
P9.timeHorizon: Community patience is thin; media will find out within 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Proactively communicate the slip to the community, framed as responsible project management
options.summary: Hold a community briefing; explain the fume treatment upgrade as a genuine environmental improvement (which it is — PM2.5 limits have been tightened). Turn the negative into a "we're doing this right" message.
options.risk: some residents and media will lead with "EAF delayed again" regardless of framing.

options.label: Say nothing until Tata makes a formal announcement
options.summary: The slip is Tata's to announce; Steve should not pre-empt.
options.risk: if it leaks first through a contractor or ITV Wales, Steve looks like he was hiding it — damaging community trust already strained by the redundancy process.

options.label: Request a Transition Board extraordinary session to discuss the implications
options.summary: Formally records that the council acted immediately on receiving the information.
options.risk: an extraordinary Transition Board session makes VO-112 a political event before the commercial dispute is even resolved.

P9.summary: The 11-week commissioning slip means the EAF jobs that people in Port Talbot were told to look forward to in "late 2027" now arrive in early 2028. For a town that lost 2,500 jobs and ~£200M in annual earnings, an 11-week slip is not a technical detail — it is another piece of bad news landing on a community that is exhausted by bad news.

P10.tension: Customer trust and contractual risk vs not triggering a commercial reaction on the basis of an unresolved dispute.
P10.timeHorizon: BMW's intelligence network will surface VO-112 within 4–6 weeks regardless.

options.label: Proactively tell all three customers about the 11-week slip immediately
options.summary: Frame it as the fume treatment upgrade — Tata is installing a better environmental system than originally specified. Show customers that Tata manages transparency well.
options.risk: BMW's procurement director may use the news to reopen the Q3 2026 grade commitment conversation; Stellantis may use it as grounds to accelerate their own alternative supplier qualification.

options.label: Hold the line — make no customer communication until Tata's official programme is formally revised
options.summary: VO-112 is an active dispute; the slip is not confirmed.
options.risk: BMW has contacts in the European steel supply chain; they will hear about it. Getting caught not telling them is worse than telling them.

options.label: Selectively communicate — tell JLR (related party, lower risk) but not BMW or Stellantis until the dispute resolves
options.summary: Manage disclosure by relationship risk.
options.risk: if BMW finds out Tata told JLR and not them, the commercial relationship damage is severe — it looks like Tata protects the related party and manages everyone else.

P10.summary: BMW's procurement director gave Priya until Q3 2026 for a grade commitment — she got through that meeting with the co-development proposal. Now, two months later, she has to decide whether to proactively tell BMW, JLR, and Stellantis about the 11-week commissioning slip before they find out from another source.

personas.label: Alun Davies
personas.name: Alun Davies
personas.role: National Officer for Steel & Metals, Community Union
personas.roleSummary: National Officer for Steel and Metals, Community Union. Member of the Tata Steel UK/Port Talbot Transition Board throughout all 15 recorded meetings. Represents steel workers nationally and specifically the Community Union membership at Port Talbot.
personas.summary: Alun Davies is named in UK Government Transition Board statements as the union representative throughout the Port Talbot transition. Community is the primary union for steelworkers, alongside GMB and Unite.
personas.background:
Alun Davies is named in UK Government Transition Board statements as the union representative throughout the Port Talbot transition. Community is the primary union for steelworkers, alongside GMB and Unite.

He navigated one of the most difficult union positions in recent UK industrial history: representing workers who overwhelmingly chose to leave (so many applied for voluntary redundancy that Tata had to refuse some applications to retain sufficient workforce for EAF operations), while also holding Tata and the government accountable for the transition promises. The Community general secretary Roy Rickhuss described the EAF groundbreaking as "bittersweet" — the phrase that defines Davies' entire posture on this project.

He previously advocated for a union-backed alternative plan that would have kept blast furnaces operational until the EAF was built — a proposal rejected by Tata in mid-2024 after months of formal consultation.

---
personas.lens: Worker welfare, community economic health, and holding institutions accountable to transition promises
contextBullets: **Union position:** Accepted the EAF path after the alternative plan was rejected; now focused on quality of transition support and future EAF employment conditions
**Membership loss:** Thousands of members left the industry through redundancy
**Current focus:** Transition fund access, reskilling access, conditions for EAF workers, Supply Chain Fund for local businesses
**Political access:** Direct line to Welsh Government ministers; named on government boards
**Legacy tension:** Was armed with a strike mandate in 2024 that the union chose not to use

kpiFamilies.label: Transition Fund access rate (% of eligible workers and businesses successfully claiming)
Reskilling programme uptake among redundant workers (count)
Re-employment rate of former Tata workers in the local economy (%)
EAF job quality — new wage rates vs blast furnace rates (% comparison)
Supply Chain Fund disbursement (£M released vs £M committed)
Local unemployment rate in Neath Port Talbot (% vs national benchmark)
Community sentiment toward the transition (qualitative survey)
EAF construction employment — local hiring rate (% local vs imported labour)

personas.decisionStyleNotes:
Alun Davies is diplomatic in formal settings but holds firm positions. He is deeply aware that his institutional credibility depends on outcomes for workers, not promises. He will not publicly criticise the EAF project — he has co-signed multiple Transition Board statements supporting it — but he will escalate hard on any commitment that is not being delivered. His decisions are shaped by the accountability he feels to the thousands of members who lost jobs during the transition.

---

*Port Talbot EAF Transformation · persona file for decision framework system*

scenarios.name: P5 scenario
scenarios.label: EAF construction workforce — local hiring rate below commitment, Tata won't move
scenarios.persona: National Officer for Steel & Metals, Community Union
scenarios.scenarioDate: April 2026 — 9 months into construction, ~20 months from commissioning target
scenarios.about:
A union accountability crisis. Tests whether the framework can handle decisions where the stakeholder's primary KPIs are qualitative (member trust, institutional credibility) and the options involve tradeoffs between short-term confrontation and long-term cooperation.

---
scenarios.scenarioTitle: EAF construction workforce — local hiring rate below commitment, Tata won't move
scenarios.scenarioBody:
The July 2025 groundbreaking was accompanied by an informal — though not legally binding — commitment from Tata Steel UK that the EAF construction programme would prioritise local labour from Neath Port Talbot and the broader South Wales region. By April 2026, the actual local hiring rate on the Sir Robert McAlpine construction programme is 34% — well below the 60%+ that Community Union and Cllr Hunt's council anticipated.

The shortfall is partly structural: specialist civil engineering and demolition trades needed for the brownfield blast furnace clearance are simply not available locally in sufficient numbers. But it is also partly commercial: Sir Robert McAlpine and its regional supply chain subcontractors have brought in cheaper labour from elsewhere.

Alun has three options:

**Option A — Escalate formally to the Transition Board.** Table the local hiring data at the next Transition Board meeting, naming the shortfall publicly and requesting Tata commit to a binding 60% local labour covenant with enforcement mechanisms. Risk: antagonises Tata and Sir Robert McAlpine at a time when Community Union needs both parties cooperative on EAF operator terms negotiations; risks being seen as obstructionist on a project the union publicly supported.

**Option B — Negotiate a quiet improvement agreement with Tata HR.** Work through Chris Jaques (P2) to agree a revised local hiring target (45–50%) and a monitoring mechanism, without publicising the current underperformance. Risk: members who lost their jobs are watching. If the data becomes public and Alun knew and said nothing, his credibility with the Port Talbot membership is gone.

**Option C — Make the data public through media, then negotiate.** Brief ITV Wales and the Senedd Research team on the local hiring figures, creating political pressure on Tata and Sir Robert McAlpine before formal negotiations. Risk: the confrontational approach damages the "bittersweet but collaborative" narrative Alun has maintained; ministers may be annoyed he didn't come to them first; Tata's cooperation on EAF operator wages and conditions becomes harder.

---
scenarios.summary: The July 2025 groundbreaking was accompanied by an informal — though not legally binding — commitment from Tata Steel UK that the EAF construction programme would prioritise local labour from Neath Port Talbot and the broader South Wales region. By April 2026, the actual local hiring rate on the Sir Robert McAlpine construction programme is 34% — well below the 60%+ that Community Union and Cllr Hunt's council anticipated.
scenarios.call: Escalate to the Transition Board, negotiate quietly with Tata HR, or go public first?
scenarios.tension:
Institutional accountability and member trust vs the cooperative relationships that Alun needs for the more consequential EAF operator employment terms negotiation in 2027.

---

decisionContext.Timehorizon: Transition Board meets in 3 weeks; ITV Wales is already asking questions
decisionContext.Stakeholders: Community Union members (especially redundant workers watching closely), Tata Steel UK CEO and CHRO, Sir Robert McAlpine, Neath Port Talbot Council, Welsh Government, UK media
decisionContext.Localhiringgap: 34% actual vs 60%+ expected; approximately 180 local jobs that should exist don't
decisionContext.Politicalcontext: Welsh Government elections approaching; local hiring in Port Talbot is a visible political issue
decisionContext.EAFoperatorcontext: Formal negotiations on EAF operator wages and shift patterns are scheduled for Q3 2026 — Alun needs Tata at the table

Community Union member trust index (pulse survey, 1–5)
Transition Fund disbursement to local workers and businesses (£M, % of commitment)
EAF operator wage negotiation status (progress score, 1–5)
Formal grievance and Transition Board escalations (count)
Media sentiment — Community Union and local workforce (positive/neutral/negative)
Welsh Government political support for union position (1–5)
Re-employment rate of redundant Tata workers in local economy (%)

options.label: Escalate formally to the Transition Board
options.summary: Table the local hiring data at the next Transition Board meeting, naming the shortfall publicly and requesting Tata commit to a binding 60% local labour covenant with enforcement mechanisms.
options.risk: antagonises Tata and Sir Robert McAlpine at a time when Community Union needs both parties cooperative on EAF operator terms negotiations; risks being seen as obstructionist on a project the union publicly supported.

options.label: Negotiate a quiet improvement agreement with Tata HR
options.summary: Work through Chris Jaques (P2) to agree a revised local hiring target (45–50%) and a monitoring mechanism, without publicising the current underperformance.
options.risk: members who lost their jobs are watching. If the data becomes public and Alun knew and said nothing, his credibility with the Port Talbot membership is gone.

options.label: Make the data public through media, then negotiate
options.summary: Brief ITV Wales and the Senedd Research team on the local hiring figures, creating political pressure on Tata and Sir Robert McAlpine before formal negotiations.
options.risk: the confrontational approach damages the "bittersweet but collaborative" narrative Alun has maintained; ministers may be annoyed he didn't come to them first; Tata's cooperation on EAF operator wages and conditions becomes harder. ---

scenarios.name: CROSS scenario
scenarios.label: The Tenova Variation Order Cascade
scenarios.scenarioDate: October 2026 — 15 months into construction, 14 months from commissioning target
scenarios.trigger: Single contract event cascading across all project roles

P1.tension: Programme continuity and 2027 date vs fiduciary discipline and legal precedent.
P1.timeHorizon: 21-day decision window; legal dispute could run 12–18 months.

options.label: Pay the full £31.4M under protest
options.summary: Programme continues uninterrupted. Legal dispute proceeds in parallel through the contract's dispute resolution mechanism.
options.risk: sets a precedent; the £14.9M may be unrecoverable if the dispute goes against Tata.

options.label: Pay only the agreed £16.5M (Factor 3)
options.summary: Dispute £14.9M through formal contract mechanism. Tenova may invoke suspension.
options.risk: programme slips 6–8 weeks on top of the 11-week extension; total slip could be 17–19 weeks; 2027 commissioning date is gone.

options.label: Negotiate a commercial settlement
options.summary: Split the difference — pay £23–25M as a full and final settlement, close the dispute, and keep Tenova cooperative for the remainder of the programme.
options.risk: paying more than Tata's legal position requires; sets precedent for future claims.

P1.summary: Tata's lawyers say withholding is defensible on Factors 1 and 2. But invoking the payment security clause would give Tenova the right to suspend construction for 6–8 weeks — and that suspension would become public. Rajesh must decide within 21 days.

P2.tension: Protecting the reskilling pipeline vs using the crisis as leverage for a structural decision the CEO needs to make anyway.
P2.timeHorizon: Next cohort starts in 6 weeks; decision needed in 10 days.

options.label: Escalate the trainer availability problem to the CEO as a commissioning risk
options.summary: Force a formal programme board decision on whether to mandate the IJmuiden secondments now, using the VO-112 evidence of industry-wide specialist scarcity as the justification. Turns a reskilling logistics problem into a strategic argument.

options.label: Absorb the 8-week delay quietly and compress later cohorts
options.summary: Manage the problem internally without escalating.
options.risk: each week of delay at this stage is harder to recover; the first cohort sets the pace for all subsequent ones.

options.label: Fast-track a direct hire of refractory specialists as EAF operator trainers
options.summary: Offer the same refractory engineers cited in Tenova's claim a parallel training role at Tata — they are on-site anyway and have the exact expertise the reskilling programme needs. Cost: ~£850K for a 6-month training facilitation contract.

P2.summary: The same refractory specialist shortage Tenova cited in VO-112 Factor 2 is being felt in Chris's reskilling pipeline — the external EAF training providers he was planning to use have had to delay Tata's cohort because their own trainers are committed to the same three simultaneous EAF projects. The reskilling timeline was already tight; the trainer availability crisis means the next scheduled cohort is now 8 weeks behind.

P3.tension: Site velocity and team relationships vs legal protection during an active commercial dispute.
P3.timeHorizon: 30-day refractory team redeployment threat; programme board meets in 5 days.

options.label: Operate the site formally under contract, documenting everything
options.summary: Issue all instructions in writing, require written acknowledgement. Protects Tata's legal position but slows decision velocity on site from hours to days.
options.risk: construction programme suffers from the bureaucratic overhead even before any formal suspension.

options.label: Maintain informal working relationship with Tenova's site team while dispute is resolved at commercial level
options.summary: Keep site velocity up; deal with the legal dispute through corporate channels without letting it infect the construction team relationship.
options.risk: informal working during a live dispute creates ambiguity about what has been agreed and for how much — any deviation becomes a new variation claim.

options.label: Accelerate parallel works that are not affected by the refractory dispute
options.summary: Shift resources to the scrap handling facility, LMF installation, and electrical infrastructure while the Tenova dispute is active. Partially decouples programme velocity from the dispute outcome.
options.risk: resource diversion may create quality risks on the accelerated work; ABB's schedule is not designed for acceleration.

P3.summary: Owen is contractually obligated to continue issuing instructions to Tenova, but Tenova's site team is operating under their own legal counsel's guidance — which means every instruction Owen issues is now being logged and potentially reserved as supporting evidence in the dispute. The site atmosphere has changed. Tenova's senior site manager has told Owen privately that if the £14.9M is not paid, the refractory team will be redeployed to the French EAF project within 30 days.

P4.tension: Cash discipline and contingency preservation vs programme continuity and grant transparency.
P4.timeHorizon: 21-day payment decision; grant covenant review cycle is quarterly.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, notify DBET proactively
options.summary: Full transparency; maintains grant relationship.
options.risk: DBET may require a formal programme review before releasing the next milestone payment — which could delay cash flow by 8–12 weeks.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, do not notify DBET unless required
options.summary: Only disclose if the overrun breaches a defined threshold in the grant agreement.
options.risk: if DBET later discovers Tata knew and didn't tell them, the relationship damage is severe.

options.label: Reject the full payment; reserve contingency for the agreed Factor 3 element only
options.summary: Do not consume contingency on disputed items. If Tenova suspends, the 6–8 week slip is a budget problem for a different period.
options.risk: programme slip costs are in some ways more expensive than the disputed amount — the indirect cost of a 6–8 week suspension (slab import extension, customer risk, staff costs) may exceed £14.9M.

P4.summary: The capital programme budget has a 7.5% contingency built in — approximately £93M against the £1.25B programme. VO-112 at £31.4M is 33% of that contingency. If Tata pays the full amount, 33% of its total programme contingency is consumed by a single contractor dispute at month 15 of a 29-month construction programme. There are 14 months left and multiple other programme risks still live.

P5.tension: Institutional accountability to members vs cooperative relationship needed for EAF operator terms.
P5.timeHorizon: Transition Board meets in 3 weeks; parliamentary question window is now.

options.label: Request a formal Transition Board agenda item on VO-112 and its workforce implications
options.summary: Forces public transparency; puts the commissioning delay and the non-local specialist labour question on the record.
options.risk: antagonises Tata at the worst possible time — when Tata needs contractor goodwill and the union needs EAF operator terms cooperation.

options.label: Accept the private briefing and monitor without escalating
options.summary: Trust the process; wait for the commercial dispute to resolve before making it a Transition Board issue.
options.risk: if the delay hardens and former workers who were counting on 2027 EAF jobs find out Alun knew and said nothing, it is a repeat of the blast furnace closure communications failure.

options.label: Raise it quietly through Stephen Kinnock (MP) as a parliamentary question
options.summary: Creates political pressure without directly confronting Tata; forces DBET to publicly confirm the programme status.
options.risk: parliamentary questions are public record; this is effectively option A but less controllable.

P5.summary: Alun will be briefed on VO-112 through his Transition Board membership. The 11-week programme extension means the EAF commissioning date is now formally at risk. This directly affects the timeline for EAF operator job creation — the moment redundant former steelworkers had been told to look forward to. The refractory labour claim (Factor 2) also highlights that Tenova is using non-local specialists for a key construction phase while Community Union has been pushing for more local hiring.

P6.tension: Board governance and director liability vs the media and political consequences of formal disclosure.
P6.timeHorizon: Legal opinion: 2–3 weeks; Indian exchange filing obligations: 48 hours if material.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as a material event and issue a proactive disclosure to DBET, Welsh Government, and Tata Steel Ltd's Indian exchange filings
options.summary: Full transparency; protects independent directors.
options.risk: disclosure triggers a media story — "Tata Steel Port Talbot EAF £31M over budget and 11 weeks late" is a damaging headline 14 months from commissioning.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as within normal programme contingency and not material — disclose only if and when the commercial dispute is resolved against Tata
options.summary: Programme-level decision; not a board-level material event.
options.risk: if the dispute escalates and it later emerges the board classified it as immaterial, independent director liability is real.

options.label: Commission a legal opinion on materiality and delay disclosure pending its conclusion
options.summary: Buys 2–3 weeks; ensures the board has defensible grounds for its decision.
options.risk: the delay itself may become an issue if DBET or Tata Group discovers VO-112 independently in the interim.

P6.summary: The Tata Steel UK board must assess whether £31.4M in disputed contract variation, plus an 11-week programme extension, constitutes a material event under UK company law, the grant agreement's disclosure obligations, or Tata Steel Ltd's listing obligations on the Indian stock exchanges.

P7.tension: Contract certainty vs the cost of acting on an unconfirmed programme slip.
P7.timeHorizon: Three scrap contracts have February 2028 termination clauses; renegotiation takes 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Renegotiate the three at-risk contracts now, before the slip is formally confirmed
options.summary: Tell the merchants the start date may move by 8–12 weeks; ask for a grace period clause in exchange for a modest price concession (~£2/tonne). Cost: ~£1.4M/yr on those contracts.
options.risk: if the programme recovers and commissioning holds at Q4 2027, Tata has paid a concession it didn't need to.

options.label: Wait for the VO-112 dispute to resolve before renegotiating
options.summary: Do not trigger a conversation with scrap merchants about a delay that has not been formally confirmed.
options.risk: by the time VO-112 is resolved (6–8 weeks), the merchants' own pricing windows may have closed — and renegotiating under time pressure will cost more.

options.label: Treat the three at-risk contracts as expendable and replace them with shorter-duration bridge contracts
options.summary: Accept the loss and sign 12-month spot-priced bridge agreements to cover the delay period. Cost: PSX spot prices are currently running ~£12/tonne above long-term contract rates.
options.risk: further delays compound the spot exposure; bridge contracts create a rolling renegotiation burden.

P7.summary: Kate's current contracting strategy assumed EAF commissioning in Q4 2027 and first full production in Q1 2028. Her long-term scrap contracts are structured around a January 2028 first delivery date. An 11-week slip pushes first production to approximately March–April 2028.

P8.tension: Contract security vs avoiding renegotiation of agreements that are currently on good terms.
P8.timeHorizon: PPA pricing trigger is Q4 2027 — formal amendment needs to be in place 8 weeks before trigger.

options.label: Issue a formal contract amendment notice to National Grid and the PPA counterparty
options.summary: Updates both agreements to reflect the revised timeline.
options.risk: formal amendments trigger renegotiation windows — National Grid may use the amendment to revise terms; the PPA counterparty may request price renegotiation on an agreement that is currently below market.

options.label: Do nothing contractually; treat both agreements as flexible within a 3-month window
options.summary: Most infrastructure contracts have implicit flexibility for minor schedule adjustments.
options.risk: if the slip exceeds the informal flexibility window and either party invokes strict contract terms, Tata is in breach — a worse position than having amended proactively.

options.label: Issue informal notice letters to both parties — acknowledging the potential slip, requesting confirmation of continued alignment, without triggering a formal amendment process
options.summary: Preserves optionality; puts both parties on notice without opening renegotiation.
options.risk: legally ambiguous — informal notice may or may not constitute sufficient notification under the contract terms.

P8.summary: National Grid committed to deliver the new high-voltage infrastructure to Port Talbot by end-2027 — aligned to the original EAF commissioning target. An 11-week slip moves Tata's requirement to approximately February–March 2028. This is actually positive for National Grid's programme (they now have more time), but it creates a contractual ambiguity: the current agreement specifies "ready by end 2027" — if Tata's commissioning moves, does the National Grid obligation move too, and does it require a formal contract amendment?

P9.tension: Community trust and transparency vs not getting ahead of a commercial dispute that may resolve in Tata's favour.
P9.timeHorizon: Community patience is thin; media will find out within 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Proactively communicate the slip to the community, framed as responsible project management
options.summary: Hold a community briefing; explain the fume treatment upgrade as a genuine environmental improvement (which it is — PM2.5 limits have been tightened). Turn the negative into a "we're doing this right" message.
options.risk: some residents and media will lead with "EAF delayed again" regardless of framing.

options.label: Say nothing until Tata makes a formal announcement
options.summary: The slip is Tata's to announce; Steve should not pre-empt.
options.risk: if it leaks first through a contractor or ITV Wales, Steve looks like he was hiding it — damaging community trust already strained by the redundancy process.

options.label: Request a Transition Board extraordinary session to discuss the implications
options.summary: Formally records that the council acted immediately on receiving the information.
options.risk: an extraordinary Transition Board session makes VO-112 a political event before the commercial dispute is even resolved.

P9.summary: The 11-week commissioning slip means the EAF jobs that people in Port Talbot were told to look forward to in "late 2027" now arrive in early 2028. For a town that lost 2,500 jobs and ~£200M in annual earnings, an 11-week slip is not a technical detail — it is another piece of bad news landing on a community that is exhausted by bad news.

P10.tension: Customer trust and contractual risk vs not triggering a commercial reaction on the basis of an unresolved dispute.
P10.timeHorizon: BMW's intelligence network will surface VO-112 within 4–6 weeks regardless.

options.label: Proactively tell all three customers about the 11-week slip immediately
options.summary: Frame it as the fume treatment upgrade — Tata is installing a better environmental system than originally specified. Show customers that Tata manages transparency well.
options.risk: BMW's procurement director may use the news to reopen the Q3 2026 grade commitment conversation; Stellantis may use it as grounds to accelerate their own alternative supplier qualification.

options.label: Hold the line — make no customer communication until Tata's official programme is formally revised
options.summary: VO-112 is an active dispute; the slip is not confirmed.
options.risk: BMW has contacts in the European steel supply chain; they will hear about it. Getting caught not telling them is worse than telling them.

options.label: Selectively communicate — tell JLR (related party, lower risk) but not BMW or Stellantis until the dispute resolves
options.summary: Manage disclosure by relationship risk.
options.risk: if BMW finds out Tata told JLR and not them, the commercial relationship damage is severe — it looks like Tata protects the related party and manages everyone else.

P10.summary: BMW's procurement director gave Priya until Q3 2026 for a grade commitment — she got through that meeting with the co-development proposal. Now, two months later, she has to decide whether to proactively tell BMW, JLR, and Stellantis about the 11-week commissioning slip before they find out from another source.

personas.label: Dr. Henrik Adam
personas.name: Dr. Henrik Adam
personas.roleSummary: Chair, Tata Steel UK Board. Vice President, European Corporate Affairs, Tata Steel Ltd (group level). Member of the Tata Steel UK/Port Talbot Transition Board. Based between Port Talbot, London, and the Netherlands.
personas.summary: Dr Henrik Adam is the named Chair of Tata Steel UK's board and simultaneously VP for European Corporate Affairs at Tata Steel group level, making him the key bridge between the Port Talbot project and Mumbai. He co-chaired the Transition Board alongside UK government ministers throughout the blast furnace closure and EAF planning approval process.
personas.background:
Dr Henrik Adam is the named Chair of Tata Steel UK's board and simultaneously VP for European Corporate Affairs at Tata Steel group level, making him the key bridge between the Port Talbot project and Mumbai. He co-chaired the Transition Board alongside UK government ministers throughout the blast furnace closure and EAF planning approval process.

He carries dual accountability: to the Tata Steel UK board (governance, grant compliance, UK regulatory and political relationships) and to the Tata Steel group (European strategic positioning, alignment with the Netherlands IJmuiden transformation, and group-level reporting to Chandrasekaran). The strategic framing he champions — that this is not a retreat but the largest UK steel investment in decades — is as much a political and investor-relations narrative as it is operational.

---
personas.lens: Board governance, political relationships, group strategic narrative
contextBullets: **Board composition:** Tata Steel UK Board includes independent members (Anne Jessopp CBE, Katherine Bennett CBE, Sarah Williams-Gardener, Jacqui Murray)
**Reporting line:** To Tata Steel group board; to UK Government on grant conditions
**European context:** Coordinates with Tata Steel Netherlands (IJmuiden DRI-EAF transition) to ensure UK and European strategies are coherent
**Political exposure:** Named in all UK Government press releases; personally responsible for political relationship with DBET, Welsh Government, and Wales Office

kpiFamilies.label: Grant covenant compliance status (UK Government — all conditions met/at-risk)
Board governance health (independent director confidence, audit committee status)
Political relationship index — UK Government and Welsh Government (1–5)
Tata group strategic alignment (EAF plan consistent with IJmuiden and India)
Reputational index — UK media sentiment on Tata Steel UK (positive/neutral/negative)
EAF project health (CEO reported summary — schedule, budget, safety)
Community Transition Board commitment delivery (% of pledges on track)
ESG disclosure compliance (UK TCFD, CSRD readiness for EAF era)

personas.decisionStyleNotes:
Dr Adam is strategic in his emotional register — he thinks in decades and in political capital, not quarters. He intervenes in operational matters only when they risk the political relationship or the grant compliance. His primary fear is an event that forces the UK government to publicly question Tata's commitment — which would destabilise the grant and potentially trigger political intervention. He is highly attuned to optics and uses institutional language precisely.

---

*Port Talbot EAF Transformation · persona file for decision framework system*

scenarios.name: P6 scenario
scenarios.label: UK Government requests a board seat — accept, negotiate, or decline?
scenarios.persona: Chair, Tata Steel UK Board
scenarios.scenarioDate: April 2026 — 9 months into construction, ~20 months from commissioning target
scenarios.about:
A board governance and political-relationship decision. Tests how the framework handles decisions where the correct answer depends almost entirely on the trust architecture between Tata, the UK Government, and the board's independent members — not on financial or operational data.

---
scenarios.scenarioTitle: UK Government requests a board seat — accept, negotiate, or decline?
scenarios.scenarioBody:
In April 2026, DBET writes formally to the Tata Steel UK board requesting the appointment of a UK Government observer to the board — a non-voting position with access to board papers and the right to attend all board meetings including any sessions discussing EAF construction progress, grant covenant compliance, and commercial strategy.

The request is framed as "in the interests of protecting the public investment of £500M." It is precedented: the UK Government holds observer positions on several other major grant-backed infrastructure projects.

Dr Henrik Adam must advise the board on how to respond. Three options:

**Option A — Accept the observer appointment unconditionally.** Full transparency; cements the cooperative relationship. Risk: Government observer has access to commercially sensitive customer negotiations (JLR, BMW, Stellantis), competitive strategy, and group-level capital discussions. Tata Steel Netherlands and India might object to a UK Government representative attending meetings where group strategy is discussed.

**Option B — Counter-propose a restructured reporting mechanism.** Offer quarterly deepened briefings for ministers, enhanced access to the Transition Board, and a dedicated government liaison officer — but without a board seat. Risk: Government may read this as Tata hiding something; the "why won't you give us a seat?" story is a bad media narrative.

**Option C — Accept a time-limited observer role for the construction phase only.** Government observer attends board meetings until EAF commissioning (end 2027), after which the arrangement is reviewed. This limits ongoing commercial exposure while satisfying the immediate accountability demand. Risk: requires a sunset clause that government may resist; creates an implicit suggestion that post-commissioning transparency decreases.

---
scenarios.summary: In April 2026, DBET writes formally to the Tata Steel UK board requesting the appointment of a UK Government observer to the board — a non-voting position with access to board papers and the right to attend all board meetings including any sessions discussing EAF construction progress, grant covenant compliance, and commercial strategy.
scenarios.call: Accept unconditionally, counter-propose an enhanced reporting mechanism, or accept with a time-limited construction-phase-only scope?
scenarios.tension:
Protecting Tata's commercial confidentiality and group interests vs satisfying legitimate public accountability for £500M of taxpayer money.

---

decisionContext.Timehorizon: DBET wants a response within 28 days
decisionContext.Stakeholders: UK Government DBET and Wales Office, Tata Steel Group board (Mumbai), independent board members (Anne Jessopp CBE, Jacqui Murray, Sarah Williams-Gardener), JLR and other customers (confidentiality concern)
decisionContext.Precedent: Observer positions exist on other UK grant-backed projects — refusal is unusual
decisionContext.Tatagroupconcern: Group board may object to a UK Government representative having access to European strategy discussions
decisionContext.Politicalrisk: Declining the request risks "Tata hides from accountability" media narrative

options.risk: each week of delay at this stage is harder to recover; the first cohort sets the pace for all subsequent ones.

options.label: Fast-track a direct hire of refractory specialists as EAF operator trainers
options.summary: Offer the same refractory engineers cited in Tenova's claim a parallel training role at Tata — they are on-site anyway and have the exact expertise the reskilling programme needs. Cost: ~£850K for a 6-month training facilitation contract.

P2.summary: The same refractory specialist shortage Tenova cited in VO-112 Factor 2 is being felt in Chris's reskilling pipeline — the external EAF training providers he was planning to use have had to delay Tata's cohort because their own trainers are committed to the same three simultaneous EAF projects. The reskilling timeline was already tight; the trainer availability crisis means the next scheduled cohort is now 8 weeks behind.

P3.tension: Site velocity and team relationships vs legal protection during an active commercial dispute.
P3.timeHorizon: 30-day refractory team redeployment threat; programme board meets in 5 days.

options.label: Operate the site formally under contract, documenting everything
options.summary: Issue all instructions in writing, require written acknowledgement. Protects Tata's legal position but slows decision velocity on site from hours to days.
options.risk: construction programme suffers from the bureaucratic overhead even before any formal suspension.

options.label: Maintain informal working relationship with Tenova's site team while dispute is resolved at commercial level
options.summary: Keep site velocity up; deal with the legal dispute through corporate channels without letting it infect the construction team relationship.
options.risk: informal working during a live dispute creates ambiguity about what has been agreed and for how much — any deviation becomes a new variation claim.

options.label: Accelerate parallel works that are not affected by the refractory dispute
options.summary: Shift resources to the scrap handling facility, LMF installation, and electrical infrastructure while the Tenova dispute is active. Partially decouples programme velocity from the dispute outcome.
options.risk: resource diversion may create quality risks on the accelerated work; ABB's schedule is not designed for acceleration.

P3.summary: Owen is contractually obligated to continue issuing instructions to Tenova, but Tenova's site team is operating under their own legal counsel's guidance — which means every instruction Owen issues is now being logged and potentially reserved as supporting evidence in the dispute. The site atmosphere has changed. Tenova's senior site manager has told Owen privately that if the £14.9M is not paid, the refractory team will be redeployed to the French EAF project within 30 days.

P4.tension: Cash discipline and contingency preservation vs programme continuity and grant transparency.
P4.timeHorizon: 21-day payment decision; grant covenant review cycle is quarterly.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, notify DBET proactively
options.summary: Full transparency; maintains grant relationship.
options.risk: DBET may require a formal programme review before releasing the next milestone payment — which could delay cash flow by 8–12 weeks.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, do not notify DBET unless required
options.summary: Only disclose if the overrun breaches a defined threshold in the grant agreement.
options.risk: if DBET later discovers Tata knew and didn't tell them, the relationship damage is severe.

options.label: Reject the full payment; reserve contingency for the agreed Factor 3 element only
options.summary: Do not consume contingency on disputed items. If Tenova suspends, the 6–8 week slip is a budget problem for a different period.
options.risk: programme slip costs are in some ways more expensive than the disputed amount — the indirect cost of a 6–8 week suspension (slab import extension, customer risk, staff costs) may exceed £14.9M.

P4.summary: The capital programme budget has a 7.5% contingency built in — approximately £93M against the £1.25B programme. VO-112 at £31.4M is 33% of that contingency. If Tata pays the full amount, 33% of its total programme contingency is consumed by a single contractor dispute at month 15 of a 29-month construction programme. There are 14 months left and multiple other programme risks still live.

P5.tension: Institutional accountability to members vs cooperative relationship needed for EAF operator terms.
P5.timeHorizon: Transition Board meets in 3 weeks; parliamentary question window is now.

options.label: Request a formal Transition Board agenda item on VO-112 and its workforce implications
options.summary: Forces public transparency; puts the commissioning delay and the non-local specialist labour question on the record.
options.risk: antagonises Tata at the worst possible time — when Tata needs contractor goodwill and the union needs EAF operator terms cooperation.

options.label: Accept the private briefing and monitor without escalating
options.summary: Trust the process; wait for the commercial dispute to resolve before making it a Transition Board issue.
options.risk: if the delay hardens and former workers who were counting on 2027 EAF jobs find out Alun knew and said nothing, it is a repeat of the blast furnace closure communications failure.

options.label: Raise it quietly through Stephen Kinnock (MP) as a parliamentary question
options.summary: Creates political pressure without directly confronting Tata; forces DBET to publicly confirm the programme status.
options.risk: parliamentary questions are public record; this is effectively option A but less controllable.

P5.summary: Alun will be briefed on VO-112 through his Transition Board membership. The 11-week programme extension means the EAF commissioning date is now formally at risk. This directly affects the timeline for EAF operator job creation — the moment redundant former steelworkers had been told to look forward to. The refractory labour claim (Factor 2) also highlights that Tenova is using non-local specialists for a key construction phase while Community Union has been pushing for more local hiring.

P6.tension: Board governance and director liability vs the media and political consequences of formal disclosure.
P6.timeHorizon: Legal opinion: 2–3 weeks; Indian exchange filing obligations: 48 hours if material.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as a material event and issue a proactive disclosure to DBET, Welsh Government, and Tata Steel Ltd's Indian exchange filings
options.summary: Full transparency; protects independent directors.
options.risk: disclosure triggers a media story — "Tata Steel Port Talbot EAF £31M over budget and 11 weeks late" is a damaging headline 14 months from commissioning.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as within normal programme contingency and not material — disclose only if and when the commercial dispute is resolved against Tata
options.summary: Programme-level decision; not a board-level material event.
options.risk: if the dispute escalates and it later emerges the board classified it as immaterial, independent director liability is real.

options.label: Commission a legal opinion on materiality and delay disclosure pending its conclusion
options.summary: Buys 2–3 weeks; ensures the board has defensible grounds for its decision.
options.risk: the delay itself may become an issue if DBET or Tata Group discovers VO-112 independently in the interim.

P6.summary: The Tata Steel UK board must assess whether £31.4M in disputed contract variation, plus an 11-week programme extension, constitutes a material event under UK company law, the grant agreement's disclosure obligations, or Tata Steel Ltd's listing obligations on the Indian stock exchanges.

P7.tension: Contract certainty vs the cost of acting on an unconfirmed programme slip.
P7.timeHorizon: Three scrap contracts have February 2028 termination clauses; renegotiation takes 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Renegotiate the three at-risk contracts now, before the slip is formally confirmed
options.summary: Tell the merchants the start date may move by 8–12 weeks; ask for a grace period clause in exchange for a modest price concession (~£2/tonne). Cost: ~£1.4M/yr on those contracts.
options.risk: if the programme recovers and commissioning holds at Q4 2027, Tata has paid a concession it didn't need to.

options.label: Wait for the VO-112 dispute to resolve before renegotiating
options.summary: Do not trigger a conversation with scrap merchants about a delay that has not been formally confirmed.
options.risk: by the time VO-112 is resolved (6–8 weeks), the merchants' own pricing windows may have closed — and renegotiating under time pressure will cost more.

options.label: Treat the three at-risk contracts as expendable and replace them with shorter-duration bridge contracts
options.summary: Accept the loss and sign 12-month spot-priced bridge agreements to cover the delay period. Cost: PSX spot prices are currently running ~£12/tonne above long-term contract rates.
options.risk: further delays compound the spot exposure; bridge contracts create a rolling renegotiation burden.

P7.summary: Kate's current contracting strategy assumed EAF commissioning in Q4 2027 and first full production in Q1 2028. Her long-term scrap contracts are structured around a January 2028 first delivery date. An 11-week slip pushes first production to approximately March–April 2028.

P8.tension: Contract security vs avoiding renegotiation of agreements that are currently on good terms.
P8.timeHorizon: PPA pricing trigger is Q4 2027 — formal amendment needs to be in place 8 weeks before trigger.

options.label: Issue a formal contract amendment notice to National Grid and the PPA counterparty
options.summary: Updates both agreements to reflect the revised timeline.
options.risk: formal amendments trigger renegotiation windows — National Grid may use the amendment to revise terms; the PPA counterparty may request price renegotiation on an agreement that is currently below market.

options.label: Do nothing contractually; treat both agreements as flexible within a 3-month window
options.summary: Most infrastructure contracts have implicit flexibility for minor schedule adjustments.
options.risk: if the slip exceeds the informal flexibility window and either party invokes strict contract terms, Tata is in breach — a worse position than having amended proactively.

options.label: Issue informal notice letters to both parties — acknowledging the potential slip, requesting confirmation of continued alignment, without triggering a formal amendment process
options.summary: Preserves optionality; puts both parties on notice without opening renegotiation.
options.risk: legally ambiguous — informal notice may or may not constitute sufficient notification under the contract terms.

P8.summary: National Grid committed to deliver the new high-voltage infrastructure to Port Talbot by end-2027 — aligned to the original EAF commissioning target. An 11-week slip moves Tata's requirement to approximately February–March 2028. This is actually positive for National Grid's programme (they now have more time), but it creates a contractual ambiguity: the current agreement specifies "ready by end 2027" — if Tata's commissioning moves, does the National Grid obligation move too, and does it require a formal contract amendment?

P9.tension: Community trust and transparency vs not getting ahead of a commercial dispute that may resolve in Tata's favour.
P9.timeHorizon: Community patience is thin; media will find out within 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Proactively communicate the slip to the community, framed as responsible project management
options.summary: Hold a community briefing; explain the fume treatment upgrade as a genuine environmental improvement (which it is — PM2.5 limits have been tightened). Turn the negative into a "we're doing this right" message.
options.risk: some residents and media will lead with "EAF delayed again" regardless of framing.

options.label: Say nothing until Tata makes a formal announcement
options.summary: The slip is Tata's to announce; Steve should not pre-empt.
options.risk: if it leaks first through a contractor or ITV Wales, Steve looks like he was hiding it — damaging community trust already strained by the redundancy process.

options.label: Request a Transition Board extraordinary session to discuss the implications
options.summary: Formally records that the council acted immediately on receiving the information.
options.risk: an extraordinary Transition Board session makes VO-112 a political event before the commercial dispute is even resolved.

P9.summary: The 11-week commissioning slip means the EAF jobs that people in Port Talbot were told to look forward to in "late 2027" now arrive in early 2028. For a town that lost 2,500 jobs and ~£200M in annual earnings, an 11-week slip is not a technical detail — it is another piece of bad news landing on a community that is exhausted by bad news.

P10.tension: Customer trust and contractual risk vs not triggering a commercial reaction on the basis of an unresolved dispute.
P10.timeHorizon: BMW's intelligence network will surface VO-112 within 4–6 weeks regardless.

options.label: Proactively tell all three customers about the 11-week slip immediately
options.summary: Frame it as the fume treatment upgrade — Tata is installing a better environmental system than originally specified. Show customers that Tata manages transparency well.
options.risk: BMW's procurement director may use the news to reopen the Q3 2026 grade commitment conversation; Stellantis may use it as grounds to accelerate their own alternative supplier qualification.

options.label: Hold the line — make no customer communication until Tata's official programme is formally revised
options.summary: VO-112 is an active dispute; the slip is not confirmed.
options.risk: BMW has contacts in the European steel supply chain; they will hear about it. Getting caught not telling them is worse than telling them.

options.label: Selectively communicate — tell JLR (related party, lower risk) but not BMW or Stellantis until the dispute resolves
options.summary: Manage disclosure by relationship risk.
options.risk: if BMW finds out Tata told JLR and not them, the commercial relationship damage is severe — it looks like Tata protects the related party and manages everyone else.

P10.summary: BMW's procurement director gave Priya until Q3 2026 for a grade commitment — she got through that meeting with the co-development proposal. Now, two months later, she has to decide whether to proactively tell BMW, JLR, and Stellantis about the 11-week commissioning slip before they find out from another source.

personas.label: Kate Ellison
personas.name: Kate Ellison
personas.role: Head of Scrap Procurement, Tata Steel UK
personas.roleSummary: Head of Scrap Procurement and Circular Supply Chain, Tata Steel UK. Reports to the CFO and COO. Based at Port Talbot with regular travel to UK scrap merchants and processors.
personas.summary: Kate Ellison is a composite persona representing the most strategically critical and least publicly visible role in the Port Talbot EAF project. She must secure approximately 2 million tonnes of high-quality UK-sourced scrap steel per year before the EAF is commissioned in late 2027 — a 70% increase in demand from a market that currently exports most of its material.
personas.background:
Kate Ellison is a composite persona representing the most strategically critical and least publicly visible role in the Port Talbot EAF project. She must secure approximately 2 million tonnes of high-quality UK-sourced scrap steel per year before the EAF is commissioned in late 2027 — a 70% increase in demand from a market that currently exports most of its material.

The UK exports 8 million tonnes of scrap annually — more than any country except the USA. Most of this is low-grade or mixed quality, processed by overseas facilities with lower costs and mature circular steel economies, and often returned to the UK as finished goods that undercut domestic manufacturers. EAFs are sensitive to contaminants (copper, tin, and residuals degrade steel quality); the scrap Kate needs is not the same scrap the UK currently sends abroad.

A December 2025 UK Steel Circular Steel Sub-Committee report warned that without investment in UK scrap processing infrastructure, the country risks being unable to secure the high-quality feedstock needed for EAF transition.

---
personas.lens: Supply security, scrap quality, circular economy development, cost
contextBullets: **EAF annual requirement:** ~2M tonnes high-quality (low-residual) scrap
**UK scrap surplus:** 8M tonnes/yr exported — but mostly lower grade
**Quality challenge:** EAF steel can contain up to 90% scrap (vs 30% in BF) but requires strict residual limits
**Competition:** Every European EAF operator is competing for the same high-quality scrap
**Infrastructure gap:** UK scrap shredding and sorting capacity is underdeveloped vs Germany, Netherlands
**Transport:** Scrap will arrive at Port Talbot primarily by rail (existing network)
**JCB commitment:** JCB signed an MOU with Tata for green steel supply — creating a commercial incentive to prioritise UK automotive scrap loops

kpiFamilies.label: Contracted scrap volume secured (tonnes, % of 2M tonne annual target)
Scrap quality compliance rate (% of deliveries meeting residual spec)
Landed scrap cost (£/tonne vs European EAF competitor benchmark)
Supplier diversity — number of accredited UK scrap suppliers
Scrap processing infrastructure investment committed (£M by third parties)
Rail logistics capacity secured (% of annual volume with confirmed rail contracts)
Import scrap dependency (% of volume from non-UK sources — target: <25%)
Circular steel loop agreements (count of customer take-back schemes signed)

personas.decisionStyleNotes:
Kate is a pragmatic operator in a market that is fundamentally relationship-driven. Scrap merchants are mostly SMEs; long-term contracts are unusual in this sector. She is building a new procurement model — treating scrap as a strategic feedstock rather than a commodity afterthought — which requires changing the mindset of both Tata and the UK scrap industry simultaneously. Her decisions tend toward strategic when negotiating infrastructure partnerships, pragmatic when making volume vs cost trade-offs.

---

*Port Talbot EAF Transformation · persona file for decision framework system*

scenarios.name: P7 scenario
scenarios.label: Long-term scrap contract — lock in now at a premium, or wait for the market to develop?
scenarios.persona: Head of Scrap Procurement, Tata Steel UK
scenarios.scenarioDate: April 2026 — 9 months into construction, ~20 months from commissioning target
scenarios.about:
A supply security decision under genuine market pressure, with an irreversibility dimension. The EAF cannot operate without scrap — this is the feedstock equivalent of the blast furnace's iron ore problem, but harder to solve because the market structure doesn't yet exist.

---
scenarios.scenarioTitle: Long-term scrap contract — lock in now at a premium, or wait for the market to develop?
scenarios.scenarioBody:
The EAF will require 2.0–2.5 million tonnes of high-quality, low-residual scrap per year from 2028. As of April 2026, Kate has contracted 820,000 tonnes per year from 11 UK scrap merchants — 41% of the requirement. The remaining 1.2–1.7 million tonnes is uncontracted.

The UK scrap market is in flux. Two dynamics are colliding:

1. **Price spike risk:** European EAF operators (Salzgitter, Thyssenkrupp, ArcelorMittal) are all competing for the same high-quality flat-steel scrap. Spot prices for prime shredded (PSX) grade are up 18% year-on-year. Locking in long-term contracts now, at today's prices, looks expensive. But if the market tightens further, the EAF's operating cost model becomes unviable.

2. **Infrastructure gap:** The December 2025 UK Steel Circular Steel Sub-Committee report warned that high-quality scrap supply at the required volume cannot be secured without investment in UK shredding and sorting infrastructure. One UK scrap group (C&B Metals, 180 employees, Hull) has approached Kate offering a 10-year offtake commitment in exchange for a £12M co-investment in a new high-specification shredder. This would guarantee 350,000 tonnes/yr of PSX-grade scrap but ties Tata to a long-term infrastructure stake.

Three options:

**Option A — Lock in long-term contracts now, accepting current premium pricing.** Contract the remaining 1.2M tonnes at spot +8–12% (£38–45/tonne above index). Provides supply certainty. Cost: ~£45–54M/yr above a hypothetical perfect-market price; adds ~£14/tonne to EAF operating cost vs plan.

**Option B — Take the C&B Metals co-investment deal.** Commit £12M capital, secure 350,000 tonnes/yr PSX-grade for 10 years at index price. Then contract remaining 850,000 tonnes at market rates. Total contracted: 1.17M tonnes + 820K existing = ~2M tonnes. Risk: £12M capital commitment needs CFO approval; Tata becomes a partial infrastructure investor in a sector it doesn't understand; if C&B Metals fails, the investment is stranded.

**Option C — Wait 12 months before contracting the balance.** Bet that the scrap market will normalise as more UK infrastructure comes online. Continue building relationships with merchants without committing. Risk: December 2027 commissioning arrives with uncontracted scrap; spot purchases in a tight market could push EAF operating costs 20–25% above plan in year 1.

---
scenarios.summary: The EAF will require 2.0–2.5 million tonnes of high-quality, low-residual scrap per year from 2028. As of April 2026, Kate has contracted 820,000 tonnes per year from 11 UK scrap merchants — 41% of the requirement. The remaining 1.2–1.7 million tonnes is uncontracted.
scenarios.call: Lock in now at a premium, co-invest in infrastructure for price certainty, or wait 12 months?
scenarios.tension:
Supply security vs operating cost discipline vs capital exposure in an infrastructure sector outside Tata's core competence.

---

decisionContext.Infrastructureoption: C&B Metals: £12M co-investment → 350,000 t/yr PSX guaranteed for 10 years
