scenarios.name: CROSS scenario
scenarios.label: The Tenova Variation Order Cascade
scenarios.scenarioDate: October 2026 — 15 months into construction, 14 months from commissioning target
scenarios.trigger: Single contract event cascading across all project roles

P1.tension: Programme continuity and 2027 date vs fiduciary discipline and legal precedent.
P1.timeHorizon: 21-day decision window; legal dispute could run 12–18 months.

options.label: Pay the full £31.4M under protest
options.summary: Programme continues uninterrupted. Legal dispute proceeds in parallel through the contract's dispute resolution mechanism.
options.risk: sets a precedent; the £14.9M may be unrecoverable if the dispute goes against Tata.

options.label: Pay only the agreed £16.5M (Factor 3)
options.summary: Dispute £14.9M through formal contract mechanism. Tenova may invoke suspension.
options.risk: programme slips 6–8 weeks on top of the 11-week extension; total slip could be 17–19 weeks; 2027 commissioning date is gone.

options.label: Negotiate a commercial settlement
options.summary: Split the difference — pay £23–25M as a full and final settlement, close the dispute, and keep Tenova cooperative for the remainder of the programme.
options.risk: paying more than Tata's legal position requires; sets precedent for future claims.

P1.summary: Tata's lawyers say withholding is defensible on Factors 1 and 2. But invoking the payment security clause would give Tenova the right to suspend construction for 6–8 weeks — and that suspension would become public. Rajesh must decide within 21 days.

P2.tension: Protecting the reskilling pipeline vs using the crisis as leverage for a structural decision the CEO needs to make anyway.
P2.timeHorizon: Next cohort starts in 6 weeks; decision needed in 10 days.

options.label: Escalate the trainer availability problem to the CEO as a commissioning risk
options.summary: Force a formal programme board decision on whether to mandate the IJmuiden secondments now, using the VO-112 evidence of industry-wide specialist scarcity as the justification. Turns a reskilling logistics problem into a strategic argument.

options.label: Absorb the 8-week delay quietly and compress later cohorts
options.summary: Manage the problem internally without escalating.
options.risk: each week of delay at this stage is harder to recover; the first cohort sets the pace for all subsequent ones.

options.label: Fast-track a direct hire of refractory specialists as EAF operator trainers
options.summary: Offer the same refractory engineers cited in Tenova's claim a parallel training role at Tata — they are on-site anyway and have the exact expertise the reskilling programme needs. Cost: ~£850K for a 6-month training facilitation contract.

P2.summary: The same refractory specialist shortage Tenova cited in VO-112 Factor 2 is being felt in Chris's reskilling pipeline — the external EAF training providers he was planning to use have had to delay Tata's cohort because their own trainers are committed to the same three simultaneous EAF projects. The reskilling timeline was already tight; the trainer availability crisis means the next scheduled cohort is now 8 weeks behind.

P3.tension: Site velocity and team relationships vs legal protection during an active commercial dispute.
P3.timeHorizon: 30-day refractory team redeployment threat; programme board meets in 5 days.

options.label: Operate the site formally under contract, documenting everything
options.summary: Issue all instructions in writing, require written acknowledgement. Protects Tata's legal position but slows decision velocity on site from hours to days.
options.risk: construction programme suffers from the bureaucratic overhead even before any formal suspension.

options.label: Maintain informal working relationship with Tenova's site team while dispute is resolved at commercial level
options.summary: Keep site velocity up; deal with the legal dispute through corporate channels without letting it infect the construction team relationship.
options.risk: informal working during a live dispute creates ambiguity about what has been agreed and for how much — any deviation becomes a new variation claim.

options.label: Accelerate parallel works that are not affected by the refractory dispute
options.summary: Shift resources to the scrap handling facility, LMF installation, and electrical infrastructure while the Tenova dispute is active. Partially decouples programme velocity from the dispute outcome.
options.risk: resource diversion may create quality risks on the accelerated work; ABB's schedule is not designed for acceleration.

P3.summary: Owen is contractually obligated to continue issuing instructions to Tenova, but Tenova's site team is operating under their own legal counsel's guidance — which means every instruction Owen issues is now being logged and potentially reserved as supporting evidence in the dispute. The site atmosphere has changed. Tenova's senior site manager has told Owen privately that if the £14.9M is not paid, the refractory team will be redeployed to the French EAF project within 30 days.

P4.tension: Cash discipline and contingency preservation vs programme continuity and grant transparency.
P4.timeHorizon: 21-day payment decision; grant covenant review cycle is quarterly.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, notify DBET proactively
options.summary: Full transparency; maintains grant relationship.
options.risk: DBET may require a formal programme review before releasing the next milestone payment — which could delay cash flow by 8–12 weeks.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, do not notify DBET unless required
options.summary: Only disclose if the overrun breaches a defined threshold in the grant agreement.
options.risk: if DBET later discovers Tata knew and didn't tell them, the relationship damage is severe.

options.label: Reject the full payment; reserve contingency for the agreed Factor 3 element only
options.summary: Do not consume contingency on disputed items. If Tenova suspends, the 6–8 week slip is a budget problem for a different period.
options.risk: programme slip costs are in some ways more expensive than the disputed amount — the indirect cost of a 6–8 week suspension (slab import extension, customer risk, staff costs) may exceed £14.9M.

P4.summary: The capital programme budget has a 7.5% contingency built in — approximately £93M against the £1.25B programme. VO-112 at £31.4M is 33% of that contingency. If Tata pays the full amount, 33% of its total programme contingency is consumed by a single contractor dispute at month 15 of a 29-month construction programme. There are 14 months left and multiple other programme risks still live.

P5.tension: Institutional accountability to members vs cooperative relationship needed for EAF operator terms.
P5.timeHorizon: Transition Board meets in 3 weeks; parliamentary question window is now.

options.label: Request a formal Transition Board agenda item on VO-112 and its workforce implications
options.summary: Forces public transparency; puts the commissioning delay and the non-local specialist labour question on the record.
options.risk: antagonises Tata at the worst possible time — when Tata needs contractor goodwill and the union needs EAF operator terms cooperation.

options.label: Accept the private briefing and monitor without escalating
options.summary: Trust the process; wait for the commercial dispute to resolve before making it a Transition Board issue.
options.risk: if the delay hardens and former workers who were counting on 2027 EAF jobs find out Alun knew and said nothing, it is a repeat of the blast furnace closure communications failure.

options.label: Raise it quietly through Stephen Kinnock (MP) as a parliamentary question
options.summary: Creates political pressure without directly confronting Tata; forces DBET to publicly confirm the programme status.
options.risk: parliamentary questions are public record; this is effectively option A but less controllable.

P5.summary: Alun will be briefed on VO-112 through his Transition Board membership. The 11-week programme extension means the EAF commissioning date is now formally at risk. This directly affects the timeline for EAF operator job creation — the moment redundant former steelworkers had been told to look forward to. The refractory labour claim (Factor 2) also highlights that Tenova is using non-local specialists for a key construction phase while Community Union has been pushing for more local hiring.

P6.tension: Board governance and director liability vs the media and political consequences of formal disclosure.
P6.timeHorizon: Legal opinion: 2–3 weeks; Indian exchange filing obligations: 48 hours if material.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as a material event and issue a proactive disclosure to DBET, Welsh Government, and Tata Steel Ltd's Indian exchange filings
options.summary: Full transparency; protects independent directors.
options.risk: disclosure triggers a media story — "Tata Steel Port Talbot EAF £31M over budget and 11 weeks late" is a damaging headline 14 months from commissioning.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as within normal programme contingency and not material — disclose only if and when the commercial dispute is resolved against Tata
options.summary: Programme-level decision; not a board-level material event.
options.risk: if the dispute escalates and it later emerges the board classified it as immaterial, independent director liability is real.

options.label: Commission a legal opinion on materiality and delay disclosure pending its conclusion
options.summary: Buys 2–3 weeks; ensures the board has defensible grounds for its decision.
options.risk: the delay itself may become an issue if DBET or Tata Group discovers VO-112 independently in the interim.

P6.summary: The Tata Steel UK board must assess whether £31.4M in disputed contract variation, plus an 11-week programme extension, constitutes a material event under UK company law, the grant agreement's disclosure obligations, or Tata Steel Ltd's listing obligations on the Indian stock exchanges.

P7.tension: Contract certainty vs the cost of acting on an unconfirmed programme slip.
P7.timeHorizon: Three scrap contracts have February 2028 termination clauses; renegotiation takes 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Renegotiate the three at-risk contracts now, before the slip is formally confirmed
options.summary: Tell the merchants the start date may move by 8–12 weeks; ask for a grace period clause in exchange for a modest price concession (~£2/tonne). Cost: ~£1.4M/yr on those contracts.
options.risk: if the programme recovers and commissioning holds at Q4 2027, Tata has paid a concession it didn't need to.

options.label: Wait for the VO-112 dispute to resolve before renegotiating
options.summary: Do not trigger a conversation with scrap merchants about a delay that has not been formally confirmed.
options.risk: by the time VO-112 is resolved (6–8 weeks), the merchants' own pricing windows may have closed — and renegotiating under time pressure will cost more.

options.label: Treat the three at-risk contracts as expendable and replace them with shorter-duration bridge contracts
options.summary: Accept the loss and sign 12-month spot-priced bridge agreements to cover the delay period. Cost: PSX spot prices are currently running ~£12/tonne above long-term contract rates.
options.risk: further delays compound the spot exposure; bridge contracts create a rolling renegotiation burden.

P7.summary: Kate's current contracting strategy assumed EAF commissioning in Q4 2027 and first full production in Q1 2028. Her long-term scrap contracts are structured around a January 2028 first delivery date. An 11-week slip pushes first production to approximately March–April 2028.

P8.tension: Contract security vs avoiding renegotiation of agreements that are currently on good terms.
P8.timeHorizon: PPA pricing trigger is Q4 2027 — formal amendment needs to be in place 8 weeks before trigger.

options.label: Issue a formal contract amendment notice to National Grid and the PPA counterparty
options.summary: Updates both agreements to reflect the revised timeline.
options.risk: formal amendments trigger renegotiation windows — National Grid may use the amendment to revise terms; the PPA counterparty may request price renegotiation on an agreement that is currently below market.

options.label: Do nothing contractually; treat both agreements as flexible within a 3-month window
options.summary: Most infrastructure contracts have implicit flexibility for minor schedule adjustments.
options.risk: if the slip exceeds the informal flexibility window and either party invokes strict contract terms, Tata is in breach — a worse position than having amended proactively.

options.label: Issue informal notice letters to both parties — acknowledging the potential slip, requesting confirmation of continued alignment, without triggering a formal amendment process
options.summary: Preserves optionality; puts both parties on notice without opening renegotiation.
options.risk: legally ambiguous — informal notice may or may not constitute sufficient notification under the contract terms.

P8.summary: National Grid committed to deliver the new high-voltage infrastructure to Port Talbot by end-2027 — aligned to the original EAF commissioning target. An 11-week slip moves Tata's requirement to approximately February–March 2028. This is actually positive for National Grid's programme (they now have more time), but it creates a contractual ambiguity: the current agreement specifies "ready by end 2027" — if Tata's commissioning moves, does the National Grid obligation move too, and does it require a formal contract amendment?

P9.tension: Community trust and transparency vs not getting ahead of a commercial dispute that may resolve in Tata's favour.
P9.timeHorizon: Community patience is thin; media will find out within 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Proactively communicate the slip to the community, framed as responsible project management
options.summary: Hold a community briefing; explain the fume treatment upgrade as a genuine environmental improvement (which it is — PM2.5 limits have been tightened). Turn the negative into a "we're doing this right" message.
options.risk: some residents and media will lead with "EAF delayed again" regardless of framing.

options.label: Say nothing until Tata makes a formal announcement
options.summary: The slip is Tata's to announce; Steve should not pre-empt.
options.risk: if it leaks first through a contractor or ITV Wales, Steve looks like he was hiding it — damaging community trust already strained by the redundancy process.

options.label: Request a Transition Board extraordinary session to discuss the implications
options.summary: Formally records that the council acted immediately on receiving the information.
options.risk: an extraordinary Transition Board session makes VO-112 a political event before the commercial dispute is even resolved.

P9.summary: The 11-week commissioning slip means the EAF jobs that people in Port Talbot were told to look forward to in "late 2027" now arrive in early 2028. For a town that lost 2,500 jobs and ~£200M in annual earnings, an 11-week slip is not a technical detail — it is another piece of bad news landing on a community that is exhausted by bad news.

P10.tension: Customer trust and contractual risk vs not triggering a commercial reaction on the basis of an unresolved dispute.
P10.timeHorizon: BMW's intelligence network will surface VO-112 within 4–6 weeks regardless.

options.label: Proactively tell all three customers about the 11-week slip immediately
options.summary: Frame it as the fume treatment upgrade — Tata is installing a better environmental system than originally specified. Show customers that Tata manages transparency well.
options.risk: BMW's procurement director may use the news to reopen the Q3 2026 grade commitment conversation; Stellantis may use it as grounds to accelerate their own alternative supplier qualification.

options.label: Hold the line — make no customer communication until Tata's official programme is formally revised
options.summary: VO-112 is an active dispute; the slip is not confirmed.
options.risk: BMW has contacts in the European steel supply chain; they will hear about it. Getting caught not telling them is worse than telling them.

options.label: Selectively communicate — tell JLR (related party, lower risk) but not BMW or Stellantis until the dispute resolves
options.summary: Manage disclosure by relationship risk.
options.risk: if BMW finds out Tata told JLR and not them, the commercial relationship damage is severe — it looks like Tata protects the related party and manages everyone else.

P10.summary: BMW's procurement director gave Priya until Q3 2026 for a grade commitment — she got through that meeting with the co-development proposal. Now, two months later, she has to decide whether to proactively tell BMW, JLR, and Stellantis about the 11-week commissioning slip before they find out from another source.

personas.label: James Whitfield
personas.name: James Whitfield
personas.role: Head of Energy & Grid Infrastructure, Tata Steel UK
personas.roleSummary: Head of Energy and Grid Infrastructure, Tata Steel UK. Reports to the CFO and to the EAF Project Director. Works daily with National Grid, UK Government energy policy teams, and energy trading desks.
personas.summary: James Whitfield is a composite persona representing the energy function that owns what may be the single biggest cost and risk variable in the EAF business case. The Port Talbot EAF will be the largest industrial electricity consumer in Wales. National Grid has a contractual commitment to build new electrical infrastructure capable of powering the 3.2 million tonne EAF by end 2027 — and James is the interface on Tata's side for that programme.
personas.background:
James Whitfield is a composite persona representing the energy function that owns what may be the single biggest cost and risk variable in the EAF business case. The Port Talbot EAF will be the largest industrial electricity consumer in Wales. National Grid has a contractual commitment to build new electrical infrastructure capable of powering the 3.2 million tonne EAF by end 2027 — and James is the interface on Tata's side for that programme.

UK industrial electricity prices are approximately 50% higher than in continental Europe — which is the primary reason European steelmakers have historically preferred operating EAFs in Germany, the Netherlands, or Spain over the UK. The UK Government committed to reducing industrial energy costs as part of the £500M grant deal. James is negotiating the detailed energy discount mechanism and a long-term Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) for renewable electricity that would both reduce cost and support the EAF's green steel credentials.

---
personas.lens: Energy security, cost competitiveness, renewable credentials, grid timing
contextBullets: **EAF power demand:** ~400MW peak draw (equivalent to a small city)
**National Grid obligation:** New infrastructure to Port Talbot by end 2027 (parallel programme to EAF construction)
**UK vs EU energy premium:** ~50% higher industrial electricity price than continental benchmark
**Government commitment:** Industrial energy discount as part of grant deal — details under negotiation
**Renewable PPA:** JLR and automotive customers expect green steel; requires renewable electricity sourcing
**Energy cost estimate:** ~£80M/yr at current UK industrial rates — the largest single operating cost of the EAF

Industrial energy price vs EU EAF competitor benchmark (£/MWh premium)
Renewable electricity PPA contracted volume (% of EAF annual demand covered)
Government energy discount — value secured (£M/yr vs baseline)
Energy cost as % of EAF operating cost model (target: below 25%)
Grid connection testing milestones (% on schedule)
Carbon intensity of electricity supply (gCO₂/kWh — affects green steel credentials)
Energy supply interruption risk (days of backup capacity)

personas.decisionStyleNotes:
James is analytically driven — the energy cost model is his primary frame for every decision. He is cautious by nature because the National Grid dependency creates irreversible schedule risk if not managed proactively. His biggest political challenge is that the UK government's energy discount commitment is real but the quantum is still being negotiated — which means the EAF business case has a known variable he cannot close. He escalates this to the CEO and CFO monthly.

---

scenarios.name: P8 scenario
scenarios.label: Renewable PPA vs grid electricity — lock in a long-term deal or wait for the government discount?
scenarios.persona: Head of Energy & Grid Infrastructure, Tata Steel UK
scenarios.scenarioDate: April 2026 — 9 months into construction, ~20 months from commissioning target
scenarios.about:
The energy cost problem is the single biggest structural risk to the EAF business case. This scenario tests the framework on a decision where the financially optimal choice conflicts with the green credentials that justify customer premiums, and where a government negotiation adds a third dimension.

---
scenarios.scenarioTitle: Renewable PPA vs grid electricity — lock in a long-term deal or wait for the government discount?
scenarios.scenarioBody:
The EAF at full operation will consume approximately 400MW peak — making it the largest industrial electricity consumer in Wales. James has been negotiating two parallel tracks:

**Track 1 — Government industrial energy discount:** UK Government committed in the grant deal to reducing Tata Steel UK's electricity costs as part of the £500M package. The specific mechanism and quantum are still being finalised. James's intelligence suggests the discount could be worth £15–22/MWh — roughly 25–35% of the current UK industrial rate — but the paperwork is not expected to be signed until Q3 2026.

**Track 2 — Renewable PPA:** A consortium of two offshore wind developers (representing 480MW of capacity in the Celtic Sea, operational by mid-2027) has offered a 15-year PPA at £68/MWh — below the current forward curve of £78/MWh. This would also allow Tata to certify the EAF's electricity as renewable-sourced, which is critical for JLR's green steel credentials and the £8–12/tonne premium customers will pay for certified low-carbon steel.

Problem: If James signs the PPA at £68/MWh and the government discount later brings the grid electricity price below £65/MWh, he has locked in a more expensive deal than the unconstrained grid option. But if he waits for the government deal to close before signing the PPA, the wind consortium may withdraw the offer.

Three options:

**Option A — Sign the PPA now at £68/MWh.** Secures renewable certification and price certainty. If the government discount makes grid cheaper, Tata absorbs the differential — estimated maximum regret: £6–9M/yr over 15 years. Also: the PPA price is below the current forward curve regardless.

**Option B — Wait for the government discount to close before deciding.** Maximum financial optionality. Risk: PPA offer expires in 8 weeks; renewable certification is lost; JLR and automotive customers may require a different certification path that is slower and more expensive.

**Option C — Negotiate a PPA with a government-discount passthrough clause.** Ask the wind consortium to include a contractual mechanism that reduces the PPA price if and when a government discount exceeds a defined threshold. Risk: wind developers are unlikely to accept full passthrough; it is a complex clause that may kill the negotiation entirely.

---
scenarios.summary: The EAF at full operation will consume approximately 400MW peak — making it the largest industrial electricity consumer in Wales. James has been negotiating two parallel tracks:
scenarios.call: Sign the PPA now, wait for the government deal, or negotiate a hybrid passthrough clause?
scenarios.tension:
Renewable credentials and price certainty now vs maximum financial flexibility pending a government negotiation that may produce a better deal.

---

decisionContext.Timehorizon: PPA offer expires in 8 weeks; government discount expected Q3 2026
decisionContext.Stakeholders: UK Government DBET (energy discount), offshore wind consortium, JLR procurement (green credentials), CFO Sian Morgan (P4), CEO Rajesh Nair (P1), National Grid
decisionContext.Energycostatstake: ~£80M/yr at current UK industrial rates; PPA would be ~£70M/yr
decisionContext.Maximumregret: £6–9M/yr if government discount makes grid cheaper than PPA
decisionContext.Greenpremiumatrisk: £8–12/tonne customer premium requires renewable electricity certification

Renewable electricity coverage (% of EAF demand from certified renewable source)

PPA terms — price vs forward curve (£/MWh differential)
Green steel certification readiness (binary — certifiable before commissioning yes/no)
National Grid infrastructure delivery (weeks of float vs EAF commissioning date)
Energy cost as % of EAF operating cost model (target <25%)
Carbon intensity of electricity supply (gCO₂/kWh — affects Scope 2 reporting)

options.label: Sign the PPA now at £68/MWh
options.summary: Secures renewable certification and price certainty. If the government discount makes grid cheaper, Tata absorbs the differential — estimated maximum regret: £6–9M/yr over 15 years. Also: the PPA price is below the current forward curve regardless.

options.label: Wait for the government discount to close before deciding
options.summary: Maximum financial optionality.
options.risk: PPA offer expires in 8 weeks; renewable certification is lost; JLR and automotive customers may require a different certification path that is slower and more expensive.

options.label: Negotiate a PPA with a government-discount passthrough clause
options.summary: Ask the wind consortium to include a contractual mechanism that reduces the PPA price if and when a government discount exceeds a defined threshold.
options.risk: wind developers are unlikely to accept full passthrough; it is a complex clause that may kill the negotiation entirely. ---

scenarios.name: CROSS scenario
scenarios.label: The Tenova Variation Order Cascade
scenarios.scenarioDate: October 2026 — 15 months into construction, 14 months from commissioning target
scenarios.trigger: Single contract event cascading across all project roles

P1.tension: Programme continuity and 2027 date vs fiduciary discipline and legal precedent.
P1.timeHorizon: 21-day decision window; legal dispute could run 12–18 months.

options.label: Pay the full £31.4M under protest
options.summary: Programme continues uninterrupted. Legal dispute proceeds in parallel through the contract's dispute resolution mechanism.
options.risk: sets a precedent; the £14.9M may be unrecoverable if the dispute goes against Tata.

options.label: Pay only the agreed £16.5M (Factor 3)
options.summary: Dispute £14.9M through formal contract mechanism. Tenova may invoke suspension.
options.risk: programme slips 6–8 weeks on top of the 11-week extension; total slip could be 17–19 weeks; 2027 commissioning date is gone.

options.label: Negotiate a commercial settlement
options.summary: Split the difference — pay £23–25M as a full and final settlement, close the dispute, and keep Tenova cooperative for the remainder of the programme.
options.risk: paying more than Tata's legal position requires; sets precedent for future claims.

P1.summary: Tata's lawyers say withholding is defensible on Factors 1 and 2. But invoking the payment security clause would give Tenova the right to suspend construction for 6–8 weeks — and that suspension would become public. Rajesh must decide within 21 days.

P2.tension: Protecting the reskilling pipeline vs using the crisis as leverage for a structural decision the CEO needs to make anyway.
P2.timeHorizon: Next cohort starts in 6 weeks; decision needed in 10 days.

options.label: Escalate the trainer availability problem to the CEO as a commissioning risk
options.summary: Force a formal programme board decision on whether to mandate the IJmuiden secondments now, using the VO-112 evidence of industry-wide specialist scarcity as the justification. Turns a reskilling logistics problem into a strategic argument.

options.label: Absorb the 8-week delay quietly and compress later cohorts
options.summary: Manage the problem internally without escalating.
options.risk: each week of delay at this stage is harder to recover; the first cohort sets the pace for all subsequent ones.

options.label: Fast-track a direct hire of refractory specialists as EAF operator trainers
options.summary: Offer the same refractory engineers cited in Tenova's claim a parallel training role at Tata — they are on-site anyway and have the exact expertise the reskilling programme needs. Cost: ~£850K for a 6-month training facilitation contract.

P2.summary: The same refractory specialist shortage Tenova cited in VO-112 Factor 2 is being felt in Chris's reskilling pipeline — the external EAF training providers he was planning to use have had to delay Tata's cohort because their own trainers are committed to the same three simultaneous EAF projects. The reskilling timeline was already tight; the trainer availability crisis means the next scheduled cohort is now 8 weeks behind.

P3.tension: Site velocity and team relationships vs legal protection during an active commercial dispute.
P3.timeHorizon: 30-day refractory team redeployment threat; programme board meets in 5 days.

options.label: Operate the site formally under contract, documenting everything
options.summary: Issue all instructions in writing, require written acknowledgement. Protects Tata's legal position but slows decision velocity on site from hours to days.
options.risk: construction programme suffers from the bureaucratic overhead even before any formal suspension.

options.label: Maintain informal working relationship with Tenova's site team while dispute is resolved at commercial level
options.summary: Keep site velocity up; deal with the legal dispute through corporate channels without letting it infect the construction team relationship.
options.risk: informal working during a live dispute creates ambiguity about what has been agreed and for how much — any deviation becomes a new variation claim.

options.label: Accelerate parallel works that are not affected by the refractory dispute
options.summary: Shift resources to the scrap handling facility, LMF installation, and electrical infrastructure while the Tenova dispute is active. Partially decouples programme velocity from the dispute outcome.
options.risk: resource diversion may create quality risks on the accelerated work; ABB's schedule is not designed for acceleration.

P3.summary: Owen is contractually obligated to continue issuing instructions to Tenova, but Tenova's site team is operating under their own legal counsel's guidance — which means every instruction Owen issues is now being logged and potentially reserved as supporting evidence in the dispute. The site atmosphere has changed. Tenova's senior site manager has told Owen privately that if the £14.9M is not paid, the refractory team will be redeployed to the French EAF project within 30 days.

P4.tension: Cash discipline and contingency preservation vs programme continuity and grant transparency.
P4.timeHorizon: 21-day payment decision; grant covenant review cycle is quarterly.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, notify DBET proactively
options.summary: Full transparency; maintains grant relationship.
options.risk: DBET may require a formal programme review before releasing the next milestone payment — which could delay cash flow by 8–12 weeks.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, do not notify DBET unless required
options.summary: Only disclose if the overrun breaches a defined threshold in the grant agreement.
options.risk: if DBET later discovers Tata knew and didn't tell them, the relationship damage is severe.

options.label: Reject the full payment; reserve contingency for the agreed Factor 3 element only
options.summary: Do not consume contingency on disputed items. If Tenova suspends, the 6–8 week slip is a budget problem for a different period.
options.risk: programme slip costs are in some ways more expensive than the disputed amount — the indirect cost of a 6–8 week suspension (slab import extension, customer risk, staff costs) may exceed £14.9M.

P4.summary: The capital programme budget has a 7.5% contingency built in — approximately £93M against the £1.25B programme. VO-112 at £31.4M is 33% of that contingency. If Tata pays the full amount, 33% of its total programme contingency is consumed by a single contractor dispute at month 15 of a 29-month construction programme. There are 14 months left and multiple other programme risks still live.

P5.tension: Institutional accountability to members vs cooperative relationship needed for EAF operator terms.
P5.timeHorizon: Transition Board meets in 3 weeks; parliamentary question window is now.

options.label: Request a formal Transition Board agenda item on VO-112 and its workforce implications
options.summary: Forces public transparency; puts the commissioning delay and the non-local specialist labour question on the record.
options.risk: antagonises Tata at the worst possible time — when Tata needs contractor goodwill and the union needs EAF operator terms cooperation.

options.label: Accept the private briefing and monitor without escalating
options.summary: Trust the process; wait for the commercial dispute to resolve before making it a Transition Board issue.
options.risk: if the delay hardens and former workers who were counting on 2027 EAF jobs find out Alun knew and said nothing, it is a repeat of the blast furnace closure communications failure.

options.label: Raise it quietly through Stephen Kinnock (MP) as a parliamentary question
options.summary: Creates political pressure without directly confronting Tata; forces DBET to publicly confirm the programme status.
options.risk: parliamentary questions are public record; this is effectively option A but less controllable.

P5.summary: Alun will be briefed on VO-112 through his Transition Board membership. The 11-week programme extension means the EAF commissioning date is now formally at risk. This directly affects the timeline for EAF operator job creation — the moment redundant former steelworkers had been told to look forward to. The refractory labour claim (Factor 2) also highlights that Tenova is using non-local specialists for a key construction phase while Community Union has been pushing for more local hiring.

P6.tension: Board governance and director liability vs the media and political consequences of formal disclosure.
P6.timeHorizon: Legal opinion: 2–3 weeks; Indian exchange filing obligations: 48 hours if material.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as a material event and issue a proactive disclosure to DBET, Welsh Government, and Tata Steel Ltd's Indian exchange filings
options.summary: Full transparency; protects independent directors.
options.risk: disclosure triggers a media story — "Tata Steel Port Talbot EAF £31M over budget and 11 weeks late" is a damaging headline 14 months from commissioning.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as within normal programme contingency and not material — disclose only if and when the commercial dispute is resolved against Tata
options.summary: Programme-level decision; not a board-level material event.
options.risk: if the dispute escalates and it later emerges the board classified it as immaterial, independent director liability is real.

options.label: Commission a legal opinion on materiality and delay disclosure pending its conclusion
options.summary: Buys 2–3 weeks; ensures the board has defensible grounds for its decision.
options.risk: the delay itself may become an issue if DBET or Tata Group discovers VO-112 independently in the interim.

P6.summary: The Tata Steel UK board must assess whether £31.4M in disputed contract variation, plus an 11-week programme extension, constitutes a material event under UK company law, the grant agreement's disclosure obligations, or Tata Steel Ltd's listing obligations on the Indian stock exchanges.

P7.tension: Contract certainty vs the cost of acting on an unconfirmed programme slip.
P7.timeHorizon: Three scrap contracts have February 2028 termination clauses; renegotiation takes 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Renegotiate the three at-risk contracts now, before the slip is formally confirmed
options.summary: Tell the merchants the start date may move by 8–12 weeks; ask for a grace period clause in exchange for a modest price concession (~£2/tonne). Cost: ~£1.4M/yr on those contracts.
options.risk: if the programme recovers and commissioning holds at Q4 2027, Tata has paid a concession it didn't need to.

options.label: Wait for the VO-112 dispute to resolve before renegotiating
options.summary: Do not trigger a conversation with scrap merchants about a delay that has not been formally confirmed.
options.risk: by the time VO-112 is resolved (6–8 weeks), the merchants' own pricing windows may have closed — and renegotiating under time pressure will cost more.

options.label: Treat the three at-risk contracts as expendable and replace them with shorter-duration bridge contracts
options.summary: Accept the loss and sign 12-month spot-priced bridge agreements to cover the delay period. Cost: PSX spot prices are currently running ~£12/tonne above long-term contract rates.
options.risk: further delays compound the spot exposure; bridge contracts create a rolling renegotiation burden.

P7.summary: Kate's current contracting strategy assumed EAF commissioning in Q4 2027 and first full production in Q1 2028. Her long-term scrap contracts are structured around a January 2028 first delivery date. An 11-week slip pushes first production to approximately March–April 2028.

P8.tension: Contract security vs avoiding renegotiation of agreements that are currently on good terms.
P8.timeHorizon: PPA pricing trigger is Q4 2027 — formal amendment needs to be in place 8 weeks before trigger.

options.label: Issue a formal contract amendment notice to National Grid and the PPA counterparty
options.summary: Updates both agreements to reflect the revised timeline.
options.risk: formal amendments trigger renegotiation windows — National Grid may use the amendment to revise terms; the PPA counterparty may request price renegotiation on an agreement that is currently below market.

options.label: Do nothing contractually; treat both agreements as flexible within a 3-month window
options.summary: Most infrastructure contracts have implicit flexibility for minor schedule adjustments.
options.risk: if the slip exceeds the informal flexibility window and either party invokes strict contract terms, Tata is in breach — a worse position than having amended proactively.

options.label: Issue informal notice letters to both parties — acknowledging the potential slip, requesting confirmation of continued alignment, without triggering a formal amendment process
options.summary: Preserves optionality; puts both parties on notice without opening renegotiation.
options.risk: legally ambiguous — informal notice may or may not constitute sufficient notification under the contract terms.

P8.summary: National Grid committed to deliver the new high-voltage infrastructure to Port Talbot by end-2027 — aligned to the original EAF commissioning target. An 11-week slip moves Tata's requirement to approximately February–March 2028. This is actually positive for National Grid's programme (they now have more time), but it creates a contractual ambiguity: the current agreement specifies "ready by end 2027" — if Tata's commissioning moves, does the National Grid obligation move too, and does it require a formal contract amendment?

P9.tension: Community trust and transparency vs not getting ahead of a commercial dispute that may resolve in Tata's favour.
P9.timeHorizon: Community patience is thin; media will find out within 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Proactively communicate the slip to the community, framed as responsible project management
options.summary: Hold a community briefing; explain the fume treatment upgrade as a genuine environmental improvement (which it is — PM2.5 limits have been tightened). Turn the negative into a "we're doing this right" message.
options.risk: some residents and media will lead with "EAF delayed again" regardless of framing.

options.label: Say nothing until Tata makes a formal announcement
options.summary: The slip is Tata's to announce; Steve should not pre-empt.
options.risk: if it leaks first through a contractor or ITV Wales, Steve looks like he was hiding it — damaging community trust already strained by the redundancy process.

options.label: Request a Transition Board extraordinary session to discuss the implications
options.summary: Formally records that the council acted immediately on receiving the information.
options.risk: an extraordinary Transition Board session makes VO-112 a political event before the commercial dispute is even resolved.

P9.summary: The 11-week commissioning slip means the EAF jobs that people in Port Talbot were told to look forward to in "late 2027" now arrive in early 2028. For a town that lost 2,500 jobs and ~£200M in annual earnings, an 11-week slip is not a technical detail — it is another piece of bad news landing on a community that is exhausted by bad news.

P10.tension: Customer trust and contractual risk vs not triggering a commercial reaction on the basis of an unresolved dispute.
P10.timeHorizon: BMW's intelligence network will surface VO-112 within 4–6 weeks regardless.

options.label: Proactively tell all three customers about the 11-week slip immediately
options.summary: Frame it as the fume treatment upgrade — Tata is installing a better environmental system than originally specified. Show customers that Tata manages transparency well.
options.risk: BMW's procurement director may use the news to reopen the Q3 2026 grade commitment conversation; Stellantis may use it as grounds to accelerate their own alternative supplier qualification.

options.label: Hold the line — make no customer communication until Tata's official programme is formally revised
options.summary: VO-112 is an active dispute; the slip is not confirmed.
options.risk: BMW has contacts in the European steel supply chain; they will hear about it. Getting caught not telling them is worse than telling them.

options.label: Selectively communicate — tell JLR (related party, lower risk) but not BMW or Stellantis until the dispute resolves
options.summary: Manage disclosure by relationship risk.
options.risk: if BMW finds out Tata told JLR and not them, the commercial relationship damage is severe — it looks like Tata protects the related party and manages everyone else.

P10.summary: BMW's procurement director gave Priya until Q3 2026 for a grade commitment — she got through that meeting with the co-development proposal. Now, two months later, she has to decide whether to proactively tell BMW, JLR, and Stellantis about the 11-week commissioning slip before they find out from another source.

personas.label: Cllr Steve Hunt
personas.name: Cllr Steve Hunt
personas.role: Leader, Neath Port Talbot County Borough Council
personas.roleSummary: Leader, Neath Port Talbot County Borough Council. Planning authority that approved the EAF planning application in February 2025. Member of the Tata Steel UK/Port Talbot Transition Board throughout all stages. Represents the community dimension of the transition.
personas.summary: Councillor Steve Hunt is named throughout UK Government Transition Board statements as the local authority leader on this project. He has a dual mandate: approving and supporting the EAF as a planning authority and community leader, while simultaneously holding Tata and the UK/Welsh Governments accountable for the economic and social impact on the 2,500+ workers and their families who lost jobs in the transition.
personas.background:
Councillor Steve Hunt is named throughout UK Government Transition Board statements as the local authority leader on this project. He has a dual mandate: approving and supporting the EAF as a planning authority and community leader, while simultaneously holding Tata and the UK/Welsh Governments accountable for the economic and social impact on the 2,500+ workers and their families who lost jobs in the transition.

He approved the EAF planning application in February 2025 with the statement that "our primary focus in the move to less carbon intensive steel production at Port Talbot has been on mitigating the effect of the net loss of jobs on our communities." Dr Calvin Jones estimated that the job losses could cost Port Talbot approximately £200 million in lost earnings — a number that directly affects local council tax revenues, high street health, and social services demand.

He has access to £100M of transition support funding (£80M UK Government, £20M Tata Steel UK) disbursed through the Transition Board, of which he is effectively the local accountability lead.

---
personas.lens: Community economic health, local employment, regeneration investment, political accountability
contextBullets: **Estimated earnings loss to local economy:** ~£200M (from ~2,500 redundancies)
**Transition fund available:** £100M (£80M UK Govt, £20M Tata)
**Supply Chain Fund:** 40 businesses employing 2,000 people began applications; eligibility widened from 50% to 30% Tata exposure
**Local unemployment:** Did not spike significantly in year 1 — attributed to transition support and diverse employment base
**Welsh Government co-funding:** Economic Growth & Investment Fund for region
**EAF construction jobs:** Local hiring rate is a live political issue

kpiFamilies.label: Local unemployment rate (% — Neath Port Talbot vs Welsh and UK average)
Transition Fund disbursement rate (£M released vs £M committed)
Supply chain businesses supported (count + jobs protected)
EAF construction — local labour hiring rate (% local vs imported workforce)
Economic Growth & Investment Fund projects initiated (count + £M)
High street and local business health index (vacancy rate, turnover)
Resident sentiment toward transition and EAF project (survey)
New employer attraction to Neath Port Talbot post-transition (count + jobs)

personas.decisionStyleNotes:
Steve Hunt operates in a deeply political environment where every decision is visible to constituents, media, and opposition councillors. His decisions are diplomatic by default — he cannot afford to publicly antagonise Tata (the only major employer) or the UK Government (the funder). But he is firm on community commitments; when the Supply Chain Fund eligibility criteria were too restrictive, he pushed for and secured a widening of the threshold from 50% to 30% Tata exposure. His primary measure of success is not the EAF itself but the community's economic trajectory around it.

---

*Port Talbot EAF Transformation · persona file for decision framework system*

scenarios.name: P9 scenario
scenarios.label: Supply Chain Transition Fund — 40 businesses waiting, money running out, criteria too narrow
scenarios.persona: Leader, Neath Port Talbot County Borough Council
scenarios.scenarioDate: April 2026 — 9 months into construction, ~20 months from commissioning target
scenarios.about:
A political and community accountability decision. Tests the framework on scenarios where the decision-maker has legitimate authority but limited direct power, and where the choice is fundamentally about the community's trust in institutions rather than operational or financial variables.

---
scenarios.scenarioTitle: Supply Chain Transition Fund — 40 businesses waiting, money running out, criteria too narrow
scenarios.scenarioBody:
The UK Government's Supply Chain Transition Fund has £30M committed (doubled in December 2024). By April 2026, 40 businesses employing approximately 2,000 people have begun the application process. However, the fund is processing slowly: only £8.2M has been disbursed, protecting approximately 390 jobs. At the current burn rate, the fund will be exhausted by October 2026 — before the majority of supply chain redundancies have been processed (most expected in 2025–2026).

Steve Hunt faces a specific problem: the eligibility threshold (30% revenue exposure to Tata, reduced from 50% in December 2024) is still too narrow for approximately 15 businesses that had 20–29% Tata exposure but have seen their Tata revenue disappear entirely because of supply chain restructuring downstream of the redundancies.

Three options:

**Option A — Escalate to the Transition Board requesting a second reduction in threshold to 20% and additional funding.** Bring it as a formal agenda item with a costed proposal (estimated additional: £8–10M to cover the 15 excluded businesses). Risk: UK Government has already doubled the fund once; a second ask may be read as poor financial management; ministers may say no at a politically awkward time.

**Option B — Create a parallel Welsh Government fund to cover the gap.** Work through Welsh Government Cabinet Secretary Rebecca Evans to create a supplementary Welsh fund for businesses in the 20–29% exposure band. Risk: Welsh Government has its own fiscal constraints; this takes 3–4 months to establish — too slow for businesses that are failing now.

**Option C — Negotiate flexible interpretation of the 30% threshold.** Work with the fund administrators to interpret "Tata exposure" to include indirect exposure (a cleaning firm that lost 28% of its revenue because its primary customer — a steel equipment supplier — went under due to the Tata closure). This could extend coverage to 10–12 of the 15 excluded businesses without a formal threshold change. Risk: flexible interpretation creates inconsistency; businesses that were refused under the strict threshold may seek judicial review.

---
scenarios.summary: The UK Government's Supply Chain Transition Fund has £30M committed (doubled in December 2024). By April 2026, 40 businesses employing approximately 2,000 people have begun the application process. However, the fund is processing slowly: only £8.2M has been disbursed, protecting approximately 390 jobs. At the current burn rate, the fund will be exhausted by October 2026 — before the majority of supply chain redundancies have been processed (most expected in 2025–2026).
scenarios.call: Escalate for more funding and lower threshold, create a parallel Welsh fund, or negotiate flexible interpretation of current criteria?
scenarios.tension:
Speed (businesses are failing now) vs political capital (asking government twice) vs legal risk (flexible interpretation invites challenge).

---

decisionContext.Stakeholders: UK Government DBET and Wales Office (Jo Stevens), Welsh Government (Rebecca Evans), 40 businesses in application pipeline, 15 excluded businesses, Neath Port Talbot residents
decisionContext.Fundstatus: £8.2M disbursed of £30M; ~390 jobs protected of 2,000 in pipeline
decisionContext.Excludedbusinesses: 15 firms with 20–29% Tata exposure; approximately 280 jobs at risk
decisionContext.Politicalcontext: Welsh Government elections approaching; community economic health is a front-page issue

This could extend coverage to 10–12 of the 15 excluded businesses without a formal threshold change.

scenarios.name: CROSS scenario
scenarios.label: The Tenova Variation Order Cascade
scenarios.scenarioDate: October 2026 — 15 months into construction, 14 months from commissioning target
scenarios.trigger: Single contract event cascading across all project roles

P1.tension: Programme continuity and 2027 date vs fiduciary discipline and legal precedent.
P1.timeHorizon: 21-day decision window; legal dispute could run 12–18 months.

options.label: Pay the full £31.4M under protest
options.summary: Programme continues uninterrupted. Legal dispute proceeds in parallel through the contract's dispute resolution mechanism.
options.risk: sets a precedent; the £14.9M may be unrecoverable if the dispute goes against Tata.

options.label: Pay only the agreed £16.5M (Factor 3)
options.summary: Dispute £14.9M through formal contract mechanism. Tenova may invoke suspension.
options.risk: programme slips 6–8 weeks on top of the 11-week extension; total slip could be 17–19 weeks; 2027 commissioning date is gone.

options.label: Negotiate a commercial settlement
options.summary: Split the difference — pay £23–25M as a full and final settlement, close the dispute, and keep Tenova cooperative for the remainder of the programme.
options.risk: paying more than Tata's legal position requires; sets precedent for future claims.

P1.summary: Tata's lawyers say withholding is defensible on Factors 1 and 2. But invoking the payment security clause would give Tenova the right to suspend construction for 6–8 weeks — and that suspension would become public. Rajesh must decide within 21 days.

P2.tension: Protecting the reskilling pipeline vs using the crisis as leverage for a structural decision the CEO needs to make anyway.
P2.timeHorizon: Next cohort starts in 6 weeks; decision needed in 10 days.

options.label: Escalate the trainer availability problem to the CEO as a commissioning risk
options.summary: Force a formal programme board decision on whether to mandate the IJmuiden secondments now, using the VO-112 evidence of industry-wide specialist scarcity as the justification. Turns a reskilling logistics problem into a strategic argument.

options.label: Absorb the 8-week delay quietly and compress later cohorts
options.summary: Manage the problem internally without escalating.
options.risk: each week of delay at this stage is harder to recover; the first cohort sets the pace for all subsequent ones.

options.label: Fast-track a direct hire of refractory specialists as EAF operator trainers
options.summary: Offer the same refractory engineers cited in Tenova's claim a parallel training role at Tata — they are on-site anyway and have the exact expertise the reskilling programme needs. Cost: ~£850K for a 6-month training facilitation contract.

P2.summary: The same refractory specialist shortage Tenova cited in VO-112 Factor 2 is being felt in Chris's reskilling pipeline — the external EAF training providers he was planning to use have had to delay Tata's cohort because their own trainers are committed to the same three simultaneous EAF projects. The reskilling timeline was already tight; the trainer availability crisis means the next scheduled cohort is now 8 weeks behind.

P3.tension: Site velocity and team relationships vs legal protection during an active commercial dispute.
P3.timeHorizon: 30-day refractory team redeployment threat; programme board meets in 5 days.

options.label: Operate the site formally under contract, documenting everything
options.summary: Issue all instructions in writing, require written acknowledgement. Protects Tata's legal position but slows decision velocity on site from hours to days.
options.risk: construction programme suffers from the bureaucratic overhead even before any formal suspension.

options.label: Maintain informal working relationship with Tenova's site team while dispute is resolved at commercial level
options.summary: Keep site velocity up; deal with the legal dispute through corporate channels without letting it infect the construction team relationship.
options.risk: informal working during a live dispute creates ambiguity about what has been agreed and for how much — any deviation becomes a new variation claim.

options.label: Accelerate parallel works that are not affected by the refractory dispute
options.summary: Shift resources to the scrap handling facility, LMF installation, and electrical infrastructure while the Tenova dispute is active. Partially decouples programme velocity from the dispute outcome.
options.risk: resource diversion may create quality risks on the accelerated work; ABB's schedule is not designed for acceleration.

P3.summary: Owen is contractually obligated to continue issuing instructions to Tenova, but Tenova's site team is operating under their own legal counsel's guidance — which means every instruction Owen issues is now being logged and potentially reserved as supporting evidence in the dispute. The site atmosphere has changed. Tenova's senior site manager has told Owen privately that if the £14.9M is not paid, the refractory team will be redeployed to the French EAF project within 30 days.

P4.tension: Cash discipline and contingency preservation vs programme continuity and grant transparency.
P4.timeHorizon: 21-day payment decision; grant covenant review cycle is quarterly.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, notify DBET proactively
options.summary: Full transparency; maintains grant relationship.
options.risk: DBET may require a formal programme review before releasing the next milestone payment — which could delay cash flow by 8–12 weeks.

options.label: Approve payment from contingency, do not notify DBET unless required
options.summary: Only disclose if the overrun breaches a defined threshold in the grant agreement.
options.risk: if DBET later discovers Tata knew and didn't tell them, the relationship damage is severe.

options.label: Reject the full payment; reserve contingency for the agreed Factor 3 element only
options.summary: Do not consume contingency on disputed items. If Tenova suspends, the 6–8 week slip is a budget problem for a different period.
options.risk: programme slip costs are in some ways more expensive than the disputed amount — the indirect cost of a 6–8 week suspension (slab import extension, customer risk, staff costs) may exceed £14.9M.

P4.summary: The capital programme budget has a 7.5% contingency built in — approximately £93M against the £1.25B programme. VO-112 at £31.4M is 33% of that contingency. If Tata pays the full amount, 33% of its total programme contingency is consumed by a single contractor dispute at month 15 of a 29-month construction programme. There are 14 months left and multiple other programme risks still live.

P5.tension: Institutional accountability to members vs cooperative relationship needed for EAF operator terms.
P5.timeHorizon: Transition Board meets in 3 weeks; parliamentary question window is now.

options.label: Request a formal Transition Board agenda item on VO-112 and its workforce implications
options.summary: Forces public transparency; puts the commissioning delay and the non-local specialist labour question on the record.
options.risk: antagonises Tata at the worst possible time — when Tata needs contractor goodwill and the union needs EAF operator terms cooperation.

options.label: Accept the private briefing and monitor without escalating
options.summary: Trust the process; wait for the commercial dispute to resolve before making it a Transition Board issue.
options.risk: if the delay hardens and former workers who were counting on 2027 EAF jobs find out Alun knew and said nothing, it is a repeat of the blast furnace closure communications failure.

options.label: Raise it quietly through Stephen Kinnock (MP) as a parliamentary question
options.summary: Creates political pressure without directly confronting Tata; forces DBET to publicly confirm the programme status.
options.risk: parliamentary questions are public record; this is effectively option A but less controllable.

P5.summary: Alun will be briefed on VO-112 through his Transition Board membership. The 11-week programme extension means the EAF commissioning date is now formally at risk. This directly affects the timeline for EAF operator job creation — the moment redundant former steelworkers had been told to look forward to. The refractory labour claim (Factor 2) also highlights that Tenova is using non-local specialists for a key construction phase while Community Union has been pushing for more local hiring.

P6.tension: Board governance and director liability vs the media and political consequences of formal disclosure.
P6.timeHorizon: Legal opinion: 2–3 weeks; Indian exchange filing obligations: 48 hours if material.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as a material event and issue a proactive disclosure to DBET, Welsh Government, and Tata Steel Ltd's Indian exchange filings
options.summary: Full transparency; protects independent directors.
options.risk: disclosure triggers a media story — "Tata Steel Port Talbot EAF £31M over budget and 11 weeks late" is a damaging headline 14 months from commissioning.

options.label: Treat VO-112 as within normal programme contingency and not material — disclose only if and when the commercial dispute is resolved against Tata
options.summary: Programme-level decision; not a board-level material event.
options.risk: if the dispute escalates and it later emerges the board classified it as immaterial, independent director liability is real.

options.label: Commission a legal opinion on materiality and delay disclosure pending its conclusion
options.summary: Buys 2–3 weeks; ensures the board has defensible grounds for its decision.
options.risk: the delay itself may become an issue if DBET or Tata Group discovers VO-112 independently in the interim.

P6.summary: The Tata Steel UK board must assess whether £31.4M in disputed contract variation, plus an 11-week programme extension, constitutes a material event under UK company law, the grant agreement's disclosure obligations, or Tata Steel Ltd's listing obligations on the Indian stock exchanges.

P7.tension: Contract certainty vs the cost of acting on an unconfirmed programme slip.
P7.timeHorizon: Three scrap contracts have February 2028 termination clauses; renegotiation takes 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Renegotiate the three at-risk contracts now, before the slip is formally confirmed
options.summary: Tell the merchants the start date may move by 8–12 weeks; ask for a grace period clause in exchange for a modest price concession (~£2/tonne). Cost: ~£1.4M/yr on those contracts.
options.risk: if the programme recovers and commissioning holds at Q4 2027, Tata has paid a concession it didn't need to.

options.label: Wait for the VO-112 dispute to resolve before renegotiating
options.summary: Do not trigger a conversation with scrap merchants about a delay that has not been formally confirmed.
options.risk: by the time VO-112 is resolved (6–8 weeks), the merchants' own pricing windows may have closed — and renegotiating under time pressure will cost more.

options.label: Treat the three at-risk contracts as expendable and replace them with shorter-duration bridge contracts
options.summary: Accept the loss and sign 12-month spot-priced bridge agreements to cover the delay period. Cost: PSX spot prices are currently running ~£12/tonne above long-term contract rates.
options.risk: further delays compound the spot exposure; bridge contracts create a rolling renegotiation burden.

P7.summary: Kate's current contracting strategy assumed EAF commissioning in Q4 2027 and first full production in Q1 2028. Her long-term scrap contracts are structured around a January 2028 first delivery date. An 11-week slip pushes first production to approximately March–April 2028.

P8.tension: Contract security vs avoiding renegotiation of agreements that are currently on good terms.
P8.timeHorizon: PPA pricing trigger is Q4 2027 — formal amendment needs to be in place 8 weeks before trigger.

options.label: Issue a formal contract amendment notice to National Grid and the PPA counterparty
options.summary: Updates both agreements to reflect the revised timeline.
options.risk: formal amendments trigger renegotiation windows — National Grid may use the amendment to revise terms; the PPA counterparty may request price renegotiation on an agreement that is currently below market.

options.label: Do nothing contractually; treat both agreements as flexible within a 3-month window
options.summary: Most infrastructure contracts have implicit flexibility for minor schedule adjustments.
options.risk: if the slip exceeds the informal flexibility window and either party invokes strict contract terms, Tata is in breach — a worse position than having amended proactively.

options.label: Issue informal notice letters to both parties — acknowledging the potential slip, requesting confirmation of continued alignment, without triggering a formal amendment process
options.summary: Preserves optionality; puts both parties on notice without opening renegotiation.
options.risk: legally ambiguous — informal notice may or may not constitute sufficient notification under the contract terms.

P8.summary: National Grid committed to deliver the new high-voltage infrastructure to Port Talbot by end-2027 — aligned to the original EAF commissioning target. An 11-week slip moves Tata's requirement to approximately February–March 2028. This is actually positive for National Grid's programme (they now have more time), but it creates a contractual ambiguity: the current agreement specifies "ready by end 2027" — if Tata's commissioning moves, does the National Grid obligation move too, and does it require a formal contract amendment?

P9.tension: Community trust and transparency vs not getting ahead of a commercial dispute that may resolve in Tata's favour.
P9.timeHorizon: Community patience is thin; media will find out within 4–6 weeks.

options.label: Proactively communicate the slip to the community, framed as responsible project management
options.summary: Hold a community briefing; explain the fume treatment upgrade as a genuine environmental improvement (which it is — PM2.5 limits have been tightened). Turn the negative into a "we're doing this right" message.
options.risk: some residents and media will lead with "EAF delayed again" regardless of framing.

options.label: Say nothing until Tata makes a formal announcement
options.summary: The slip is Tata's to announce; Steve should not pre-empt.
options.risk: if it leaks first through a contractor or ITV Wales, Steve looks like he was hiding it — damaging community trust already strained by the redundancy process.

options.label: Request a Transition Board extraordinary session to discuss the implications
options.summary: Formally records that the council acted immediately on receiving the information.
options.risk: an extraordinary Transition Board session makes VO-112 a political event before the commercial dispute is even resolved.

P9.summary: The 11-week commissioning slip means the EAF jobs that people in Port Talbot were told to look forward to in "late 2027" now arrive in early 2028. For a town that lost 2,500 jobs and ~£200M in annual earnings, an 11-week slip is not a technical detail — it is another piece of bad news landing on a community that is exhausted by bad news.

P10.tension: Customer trust and contractual risk vs not triggering a commercial reaction on the basis of an unresolved dispute.
P10.timeHorizon: BMW's intelligence network will surface VO-112 within 4–6 weeks regardless.

options.label: Proactively tell all three customers about the 11-week slip immediately
options.summary: Frame it as the fume treatment upgrade — Tata is installing a better environmental system than originally specified. Show customers that Tata manages transparency well.
options.risk: BMW's procurement director may use the news to reopen the Q3 2026 grade commitment conversation; Stellantis may use it as grounds to accelerate their own alternative supplier qualification.

options.label: Hold the line — make no customer communication until Tata's official programme is formally revised
options.summary: VO-112 is an active dispute; the slip is not confirmed.
options.risk: BMW has contacts in the European steel supply chain; they will hear about it. Getting caught not telling them is worse than telling them.

options.label: Selectively communicate — tell JLR (related party, lower risk) but not BMW or Stellantis until the dispute resolves
options.summary: Manage disclosure by relationship risk.
options.risk: if BMW finds out Tata told JLR and not them, the commercial relationship damage is severe — it looks like Tata protects the related party and manages everyone else.

P10.summary: BMW's procurement director gave Priya until Q3 2026 for a grade commitment — she got through that meeting with the co-development proposal. Now, two months later, she has to decide whether to proactively tell BMW, JLR, and Stellantis about the 11-week commissioning slip before they find out from another source.

personas.label: Priya Mehta
personas.name: Priya Mehta
personas.roleSummary: Commercial Director, Tata Steel UK — Automotive and Advanced Manufacturing. Reports to the CEO. Owns the customer-facing revenue side of the EAF business case: securing long-term offtake agreements that justify the investment and maintaining continuity through the 2025–2027 transition gap.
personas.summary: Priya Mehta is a composite persona representing the commercial function that is, in many ways, the ultimate test of the entire £1.25B investment. The EAF produces financial returns only if customers buy the steel it makes — at a price that covers operating costs, repays capital, and provides a margin.
personas.background:
Priya Mehta is a composite persona representing the commercial function that is, in many ways, the ultimate test of the entire £1.25B investment. The EAF produces financial returns only if customers buy the steel it makes — at a price that covers operating costs, repays capital, and provides a margin.

JLR (Jaguar Land Rover) has already signed a memorandum of understanding with Tata Steel for green steel supply from Port Talbot after the EAF transformation — the most significant commercial signal from the project. Priya's job is to convert that MOU into a signed multi-year offtake agreement, and to replicate it with BMW, Stellantis, and aerospace customers before 2027.

The steel grade challenge is live: EAF steel is technically suitable for approximately 90% of automotive grades. The remaining 10% — the most demanding electrical steels, ultra-high-strength automotive grades, and certain packaging steels — still require a virgin iron source, which the EAF alone cannot provide without adding direct reduced iron (DRI/HBI). Priya is selling what the EAF can do while managing customer expectations on what it cannot do yet.

---
personas.lens: Customer retention, product quality confidence, green steel premium realisation
contextBullets: **JLR:** MOU signed for green steel post-2027; Tata Motors owns JLR — a related-party dynamic
**BMW:** UK plants (MINI, Rolls-Royce) are significant flat steel customers
**Stellantis:** Already pushed back on price increases during transition gap
**Packaging customers:** DS Smith, Crown, Rexnord — require tinplate grades
**Construction:** Significant volume but lower grade requirements; less green-steel premium
**Transition gap:** 2025–2027 — customers supplied from imported Dutch/Indian slab at cost premium
**Green steel premium:** JCB has also signed an MOU — signals industrial customers will pay a premium for verified low-carbon steel

kpiFamilies.label: Offtake volume contracted for post-2027 EAF steel (MT, % of 3.2M tonne capacity)
JLR MOU conversion to signed offtake agreement (binary milestone + terms)
Transition gap customer retention rate (% of pre-transition revenue maintained)
Green steel premium realised (£/tonne above standard grade price)
Automotive grade coverage — % of customer spec grades achievable with EAF steel
Transition gap margin — imported slab cost vs customer price (£/tonne spread)
New customer pipeline — non-automotive green steel enquiries (volume + sector)
Customer NPS — confidence in 2027 delivery and grade capability (1–100)

personas.decisionStyleNotes:
Priya is strategically minded but commercially pragmatic. She knows that the JLR MOU is a gift — the related-party relationship means it will convert to a contract — but that every other customer relationship has to be earned purely on commercial merit. She is cautious about overpromising on grade capability before the EAF is commissioned and the metallurgical programme is validated. Her biggest risk is that a competitor (British Steel Scunthorpe, continental producers) picks off a key automotive customer during the 2025–2027 gap when Tata UK cannot supply from domestic primary production.

---

*Port Talbot EAF Transformation · persona file for decision framework system*

scenarios.name: P10 scenario
scenarios.label: BMW demands grade commitment before 2027 — can the EAF deliver AHSS or not?
scenarios.persona: Commercial Director, Tata Steel UK
scenarios.scenarioDate: April 2026 — 9 months into construction, ~20 months from commissioning target
scenarios.about:
The ultimate test of the EAF's commercial rationale. A scenario where the product capability gap between what the EAF can make and what the most valuable customers need is real, narrowing, but not yet closed — and a customer is forcing the issue before Tata is ready to answer.

---
scenarios.scenarioTitle: BMW demands grade commitment before 2027 — can the EAF deliver AHSS or not?
scenarios.scenarioBody:
BMW UK (MINI, Rolls-Royce) is Tata Steel UK's second-largest automotive customer after JLR. In April 2026, BMW's procurement director delivers a formal request: Tata must confirm by Q3 2026 whether the Port Talbot EAF will be able to supply Advanced High-Strength Steel (AHSS) grades for MINI body panels — specifically DP1000 and DP1200 grades — when the EAF is commissioned.

If Tata cannot confirm this by Q3, BMW will begin a qualification process with an alternative European supplier. This would represent approximately £48M/yr in annual revenue from 2028 — 8% of the EAF's revenue target.

The problem: the EAF's ability to produce DP1000/1200 grades depends on scrap quality (copper residuals must be below 0.08% for these grades — tighter than the standard PSX spec Kate Ellison is contracting). Tata's metallurgical team believes the ladle metallurgy furnaces (LMFs) under construction will achieve this spec, but the commissioning tests won't be complete until Q2 2028. There is no confirmed data.

Three options:

**Option A — Confirm the commitment to BMW unconditionally.** Tell BMW DP1000/DP1200 will be available from EAF commissioning. Keep the customer; accelerate the metallurgical validation programme internally. Risk: if the LMF commissioning tests fail to achieve the required residuals, Tata has committed to a product it cannot deliver; contract penalties and customer relationship damage follow.

**Option B — Decline to confirm and risk losing BMW.** Give BMW an honest answer: Tata cannot guarantee AHSS grades until Q2 2028 metallurgical validation is complete. Offer transitional supply from IJmuiden in the interim. Risk: BMW begins competitive qualification; Priya loses the account; £48M/yr revenue disappears from the EAF business case; sends a signal to other automotive customers about EAF grade capability.

**Option C — Commit to a phased qualification programme with BMW jointly.** Propose a co-development programme: BMW's metallurgical team and Tata's work together on the LMF commissioning spec from Q3 2026, with BMW given first sight of validation data in Q2 2028 and a right of first refusal on AHSS supply. BMW shares the grade development risk; Tata keeps the relationship without an unconditional commitment. Risk: BMW may decline the co-development offer and demand a straight yes/no anyway.

---
scenarios.summary: BMW UK (MINI, Rolls-Royce) is Tata Steel UK's second-largest automotive customer after JLR. In April 2026, BMW's procurement director delivers a formal request: Tata must confirm by Q3 2026 whether the Port Talbot EAF will be able to supply Advanced High-Strength Steel (AHSS) grades for MINI body panels — specifically DP1000 and DP1200 grades — when the EAF is commissioned.
scenarios.call: Commit unconditionally, decline and manage the loss, or propose a joint qualification programme?
scenarios.tension:
Revenue security and customer retention vs technical honesty about a product capability gap that exists — and may be resolved, but only after commissioning.

---

decisionContext.Timehorizon: BMW wants an answer by Q3 2026; EAF validation data available Q2 2028
decisionContext.Revenueatrisk: £48M/yr from BMW AHSS grades — 8% of EAF annual revenue target
decisionContext.Technicaluncertainty: DP1000/DP1200 requires copper residuals <0.08%; LMF capability unproven until 2028
decisionContext.Stakeholders: BMW procurement director, JLR (watching), Tata Steel UK metallurgical team, CFO Sian Morgan (P4), CEO Rajesh Nair (P1), Tata Steel Netherlands (could supply AHSS interim)
decisionContext.Precedentrisk: How Tata handles BMW's question will become known to Stellantis and other AHSS customers

AHSS-capable grade coverage — % of customer portfolio achievable with EAF+LMF (%)
BMW account revenue — contracted vs at-risk (£M/yr)
LMF metallurgical validation programme status (milestone % complete)
Customer NPS — automotive accounts (confidence in 2027/28 delivery and grade spec)
Green steel premium realised — automotive (£/tonne vs plan)
Transition gap revenue — downstream mills (% of pre-transition automotive revenue retained)
Competitive qualification activity — customers beginning alternative supplier qualification (count)
